Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, loss, longing, and the enduring nature of affection, along with their emotional weight and poetic devices:

---

**1.**  
*When your sweet issue your sweet form should bear,*  
*Presume not on thy heart when mine is slain;*  
*Flatter the mountain-tops with sovereign eye,*  
*Making a famine where abundance lies,*  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*As any mother's child, though not so bright*  
*Is from the book of honour razed quite,*  
*But if the while I think on thee, dear friend,*  
*Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard,*  
*As he takes from you, I engraft you new.*  

**2.**  
*So I, made lame by fortune's dearest spite,*  
*And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
*Those hours, that with gentle work did frame*  
*And from the forlorn world his visage hide,*  
*To find where your true image pictured lies;*  
*No love toward others in that bosom sits*  
*With ugly rack on his celestial face,*  
*And many maiden gardens yet unset*  
*Nor can thy shame give physic to my grief;*  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy.*  

**3.**  
*Why lovest thou that which thou receivest not gladly,*  
*Gilding the object whereupon it gazeth;*  
*That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:*  
*In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear.*  

**4.**  
*But that I hope some good conceit of thine*  
*To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way,*  
*The age to come would say 'This poet lies:'*  
*The painful warrior famoused for fight,*  
*I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought,*  
*As tender nurse her babe from faring ill.*  

**5.**  
*In our two loves there is but one respect,*  
*Even so my sun one early morn did shine*  
*Thou of thyself thy sweet self dost deceive.*  
*That heaven's air in this huge rondure hems.*  

**6.**  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*  
*For precious friends hid in death's dateless night,*  
*And all the rest forgot for which he toil'd:*  
*With all triumphant splendor on my brow.*  

**7.**  
*Make sweet some vial; treasure thou some place*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  

**8.**  
*They draw but what they see, know not the heart.*  
*As, thou being mine, mine is thy good report.*  

**9.**  
*Yet doth it steal sweet hours from love's delight.*  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*  
*For precious friends hid in death's dateless night,*  
*And all the rest forgot for which he toil'd:*  
*With all triumphant splendor on my brow.*  

**10.**  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live,*  
*This wish I have; then ten times happy me!*  

**11.**  
*FROM fairest creatures we desire increase,*  
*Who with his fear is put besides his part,*  
*The bounteous largess given thee to give?*  

**12.**  
*To see his active child do deeds of youth,*  
*To witness duty, not to show my wit:*  
*Is but the seemly raiment of my heart,*  
*Hast thou, the master-mistress of my passion.*  

**13.**  
*That's for thyself to breed another thee,*  
*Harsh featureless and rude, barrenly perish:*  
*Attending on his golden pilgrimage;*  
*To the wide world and all her fading sweets.*  

**14.**  
*Nor can thy shame give physic to my grief;*  
*And for a woman wert thou first created,*  
*Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,*  
*How many a holy and obsequious tear*  
*That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows.*  

**15.**  
*When I do count the clock that tells the time,*  
*As fast as thou shalt wane, so fast thou growest*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  
*That thou no form of thee hast left behind,*  
*Which husbandry in honour might uphold.*  

**16.**  
*By children's eyes her husband's shape in mind.*  

**17.**  
*Of his self-love, to stop posterity?*  
*But do not so; I love thee in such sort*  
*Make war upon this bloody tyrant, Time?*  
*Die single, and thine image dies with thee.*  

**18.**  
*These poor rude lines of thy deceased lover,*  
*Cheered and cheque'd even by the self-same sky,*  
*Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,*  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*For shame! deny that thou bear'st love to any,*  
*And moan the expense of many a vanish'd sight:*  
*To the wide world and all her fading sweets.*  

**19.**  
*So long lives this and this gives life to thee.*  

**20.**  
*And trouble deal heaven with my bootless cries*  
*If thou couldst answer 'This fair child of mine'*  
*If thou survive my well-contented day,*  
*Where all the treasure of thy lusty days,*  
*For never-resting time leads summer on*  
*Their images I loved I view in thee,*  
*Who with his fear is put besides his part,*  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy.*  

---

These lines highlight the poem’s themes of love, longing, and the enduring power of affection, using metaphors like "the grave where buried love doth live" and "heaven's air in this huge rondure hems." They also reflect the speaker’s emotional struggle with loss and the cyclical nature of love.<|im_end|>

**1.**  
*Entitled in thy parts do crowned sit,

**2.**  
*So shall those blots that do with me remain*  
*In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear.*  

**3.**  
*But when in thee time's furrows I behold,*  
*Be, as thy presence is, gracious and kind,*  
*When every private widow well may keep*

*O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might.*  

**5.**  
*The age to come would say 'This poet lies:'*

*So should the lines of life that life repair,*  
*And puts apparel on my tatter'd loving,*  
*Holds in perfection but a little moment.*  

**8.**

*Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing:*  
*That then I scorn to change my state with kings.*  

**9.**

*Shall sum my count and make my old excuse,*  
*Which to repair should be thy chief desire.*  

**10.**  
*By chance or nature's changing course untrimm'd;*  
*The one by toil, the other to complain*  
*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings*  
*And loathsome canker lives in sweetest bud.*  

**11.**  
*Much steals men's eyes and women's souls amazeth.*  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident:*  
*But as the marigold at the sun's eye,*  
*So shall those blots that do with me remain.*

*Or who is he so fond will be the tomb*  
*I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought,*  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*By unions married, do offend thine ear,*  
*Proving his beauty by succession thine!*  

**13.**  
*But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,*  
*The painful warrior famoused for fight,*  
*Within thine own bud buriest thy content*  
*Against the stormy gusts of winter's day.*  

**14.**  
*Which bounteous gift thou shouldst in bounty cherish:*  
*Where wasteful Time debateth with Decay,*  
*Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?*  

**15.**  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase,*  
*Herein lives wisdom, beauty and increase:*  
*These poor rude lines of thy deceased lover,*  
*Thou mayst call thine when thou from youth convertest.*  

**16.**  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident:*  
*His tender heir might bear his memory:*  
*Make glad and sorry seasons as thou fleets,*  
*But when from highmost pitch, with weary car,*  
*Make glad and sorry seasons as thou fleets,*  
*Whereon the stars in secret influence comment:*  

**17.**  
*Find no determination: then you were*  
*So then I am not lame, poor, nor despised,*  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
*With virtuous wish would bear your living flowers,*  
*If thou couldst answer 'This fair child of mine'*  
*If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:*  

**18.**  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
*Such civil war is in my love and hate*  
*When every private widow well may keep*  
*For thou art so possess'd with murderous hate*  
*Or who is he so fond will be the tomb*  
*Who all their parts of me to thee did give:*  

**19.**  
*Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart,*  
*Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard,*  
*Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow,*  
*And that unfair which fairly doth excel:*  

**20.**  
*Not from the stars do I my judgment pluck;*  
*Beauty o'ersnow'd and bareness every where:*

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, self-reflection, and the struggle against time:

1. **Love and Beauty**  
   *"O, learn to read what silent love hath writ;*  
   *"Thy beauty's form in table of my heart;*  
   *"I will not praise that purpose not to sell."*  

2. **Self-Reflection and Conflict**  
   *"Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue,*  
   *"Him in thy course untainted do allow*  
   *"That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace."*  

3. **Nature and Time**  
   *"The summer's green all girded up in sheaves,*  
   *"Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*  
   *"Thyself thy sweet self dost deceive."*  

4. **Desire for Beauty**  
   *"From fairest creatures we desire increase,*  
   *"So should the lines of life that life repair."*  

5. **Metaphors of Love**  
   *"Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind,*  
   *"Can make you live yourself in eyes of men."*  
   *"And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
   *"Leaving thee living in posterity?"*  

6. **Internal Conflict**  
   *"Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,*  
   *"Within thine own bud buriest thy content."*  

7. **Fate and Mortality**  
   *"For at a frown they in their glory die.*  
   *"When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,*  
   *"Without thy help by me be borne alone."*  

8. **Hope and Longing**  
   *"If all were minded so, the times should cease,*  
   *"But that I hope some good conceit of thine*  
   *"Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel."*  

9. **Nature’s Imagery**  
   *"As the marigold at the sun's eye,*  
   *"That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows."*  

10. **Final Reflection**  
    *"And only herald to the gaudy spring,*  
    *"That heaven's air in this huge rondure hems."*  
    *"Be not self-will'd, for thou art much too fair,*  
    *"And having climb'd the steep-up heavenly hill."*  

These lines encapsulate the poem’s themes of love, beauty, self-doubt, and the tension between mortality and eternal longing. They highlight the speaker’s yearning for connection, the paradox of beauty’s fleeting nature, and the enduring power of love.<|im_end|>

**1.**  
*Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,*  
*And from the forlorn world his visage hide,*  
*Delights to peep, to gaze therein on thee;*  
*Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most.*  

**2.**  
*Thy beauty's form in table of my heart,*  
*The offender's sorrow lends but weak relief.*  

**3.**  
*Let me confess that we two must be twain,*  
*And threescore year would make the world away.*  

**4.**  
*My love shall in my verse ever live young.*  

**5.**  
*Thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*So long as youth and thou are of one date.*  

**6.**  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*Entitled in thy parts do crowned sit.*  

**7.**  
*Feed'st thy light'st flame with self-substantial fuel,*  
*And weep afresh love's long since cancell'd woe.*  

**8.**  
*But thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*Nature's bequest gives nothing but doth lend.*  

**9.**  
*Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit,*  
*Then being ask'd where all thy beauty lies,*  
*I may not evermore acknowledge thee.*  

**10.**  
*O, none but unthrifts! Dear my love, you know*  
*What acceptable audit canst thou leave?*  

**11.**  
*The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now,*  
*And perspective it is the painter's art.*  

**12.**  
*So long as youth and thou are of one date,*  
*Thy eternal summer shall not fade.*  

**13.**  
*The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell,*  
*Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?*  

**14.**  
*But wherefore do not you a mightier way*  
*This wish I have; then ten times happy me!*  

**15.**  
*From sullen earth, sings hymns at heaven's gate,*  
*Within thine own bud buriest thy content.*  

**16.**  
*Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,*  
*For having traffic with thyself alone.*  

**17.**  
*Reserve them for my love, not for their rhyme,*  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite.*  

**18.**  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*Then of thy beauty do I question make.*  

**19.**  
*Thy unused beauty must be tomb'd with thee,*  
*Unthrifty loveliness, why dost thou spend?*  

**20.**  
*Die single, and thine image dies with thee.*  

---

These lines emphasize the **eternal nature of love**, the **transcendence of beauty**, and the **speaker's devotion** despite time and separation. They highlight themes of **timelessness**, **unyielding affection**, and the **enduring legacy of love**.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, struggle, and the enduring nature of affection, along with their thematic significance:

---

**1.**  
*Is but the seemly raiment of my heart,*  
*Or say with princes if it shall go well,*  
*Weary with toil, I haste me to my bed,*  
*When lofty trees I see barren of leaves.*  

**2.**  
*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings,*  
*She carved thee for her seal, and meant thereby*  
*Entitled in thy parts do crowned sit,*  
*Is it for fear to wet a widow's eye.*  

**3.**  
*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*When, in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes,*  
*As fast as thou shalt wane, so fast thou growest.*  

**4.**  
*And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*  
*Shifts but his place, for still the world enjoys it.*

*I make my love engrafted to this store:*  
*When day's oppression is not eased by night,*  
*Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate.*

*Hung with the trophies of my lovers gone,*  
*So long lives this and this gives life to thee.*

*Who with his fear is put besides his part,*  
*To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir.*

*That thereby beauty's rose might never die,*  
*Herein lives wisdom, beauty and increase.*

*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings,*  
*Who all their parts of me to thee did give,*  
*I tell the day, to please them thou art bright.*

*The dear repose for limbs with travel tired,*  
*Find no determination: then you were*  
*Pity the world, or else this glutton be,*  
*And look upon myself and curse my fate.*

*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*When, in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes,*  
*As fast as thou shalt wane, so fast thou growest.*

*And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*  
*Shifts but his place, for still the world enjoys it.*

*For no man well of such a salve can speak*  
*The one by toil, the other to complain*  
*That thereby beauty's rose might never die.*

*So is it not with me as with that Muse*  
*That use is not forbidden usury,*  
*But since he died and poets better prove,*  
*Mine be thy love and thy love's use their treasure.*

8. **"Now stand you on the top of happy hours, For thee and for myself no quiet find."**

10. **"Lord of my love, to whom in vassalage Yet him for this my love no whit disdaineth;"**  
11. **"And puts apparel on my tatter'd loving, That on himself such murderous shame commits."**  
12. **"Stirr'd by a painted beauty to his verse, O' let me, true in love, but truly write;"**  
13. **"Which I by lacking have supposed dead, And that fresh blood which youngly thou bestowest;"**  
14. **"And see the brave day sunk in hideous night; Now stand you on the top of happy hours, To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir."**  
15. **"Pity the world, or else this glutton be, My love shall in my verse ever live young."**  
16. **"Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy? Intend a zealous pilgrimage to thee, The bounteous largess given thee to give?"**  
17. **"Where all the treasure of thy lusty days, Look I death my days should expiate."**

24. **"Calls back the lovely April of her prime: I make my love engrafted to this store: Hiding thy bravery in their rotten smoke? Thou mayst call thine when thou from youth convertest. And moan the expense of many a vanish'd sight: Then look I death my days should expiate."**

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, sacrifice, and enduring devotion, along with their significance:

---

**Lines with thematic depth and emotional resonance:**

1. **"To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way, / To show me worthy of thy sweet respect:"**  
   *Themes: Sacrifice, devotion, and the struggle to be seen as worthy.*

2. **"Kissing with golden face the meadows green, / That thou consumest thyself in single life?"**  
   *Themes: Selflessness, the cost of love, and the lover’s willingness to endure.*

3. **"I will not praise that purpose not to sell. / All losses are restored and sorrows end."**  
   *Themes: The futility of praise, the illusion of perfection, and the promise of renewal.*

4. **"Sweets with sweets war not, joy delights in joy."**  
   *Themes: Enduring love, the triumph of joy over sorrow, and the paradox of beauty.*

5. **"But flowers distill'd though they with winter meet, / Let me confess that we two must be twain."**  
   *Themes: Separation as a necessary part of love, the inevitability of distance.*

6. **"And yet methinks I have astronomy, / Lord of my love, to whom in vassalage / And kept unused, the user so destroys it."**  
   *Themes: The lover’s yearning for autonomy, the paradox of being both servant and sovereign.*

7. **"O, learn to read what silent love hath writ: / Which in thy breast doth live, as thine in me:"**  
   *Themes: Love as a silent, enduring force, the lover’s recognition of their own devotion.*

8. **"Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age, / Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother."**  
   *Themes: The lover’s self-doubt, the fear of being seen as insincere, and the burden of expectation.*

9. **"If the true concord of well-tuned sounds, / If I could write the beauty of your eyes / The world will be thy widow and still weep."**  
   *Themes: The lover’s desire to be seen as beautiful, the fear of being unloved, and the cost of admiration.*

10. **"But I forbid thee one most heinous crime: / In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear."**  
    *Themes: The lover’s refusal to compromise their own autonomy, the moral weight of sacrifice.*

11. **"Entitled in thy parts do crowned sit, / Nor can I fortune to brief minutes tell, / Do in consent shake hands to torture me;"**  
    *Themes: The lover’s assertion of self, the pain of mutual suffering, and the power of love.*

12. **"And all those friends which I thought buried. / Whose strength's abundance weakens his own heart."**  
    *Themes: The lover’s internal conflict, the paradox of love’s burden, and the duality of self.*

13. **"O' let me, true in love, but truly write, / Him in thy course untainted do allow / Till then not show my head where thou mayst prove me."**  
    *Themes: The lover’s insistence on sincerity, the fear of betrayal, and the need for trust.*

---

**Lines with vivid imagery and metaphor:**

14. **"Hung with the trophies of my lovers gone, / Wishing me like to one more rich in hope, / And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:"**  
   *Themes: The lover’s longing for connection, the weight of memory, and the passage of time.*

15. **"If I could write the beauty of your eyes / With sun and moon, with earth and sea's rich gems, / And shalt by fortune once more re-survey / That I an accessary needs must be."**  
   *Themes: The lover’s yearning for perfection, the illusion of beauty, and the desire for recognition.*

16. **"A summer's day? Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing?"**  
   *Themes: The lover’s rejection of superficial beauty, the paradox of love’s true essence.*

---

**Lines that highlight the struggle between self and love:**

17. **"To march in ranks of better equipage: / Who will believe my verse in time to come, / Which bounteous gift thou shouldst in bounty cherish:"**  
   *Themes: The lover’s plea for recognition, the fear of being forgotten, and the value of love.*

18. **"If thou survive my well-contented day, / The offender's sorrow lends but weak relief / The dear repose for limbs with travel tired; / Attending on his golden pilgrimage;"**  
   *Themes: The lover’s selflessness, the burden of care, and the paradox of love’s cost.*

---

These lines encapsulate the poem’s exploration of love as a transformative, often painful, and enduring force. They highlight themes of sacrifice, selflessness, and the duality of beauty and suffering. The repetition of motifs like "sweets with sweets war not" and the contrast between idealized beauty and reality underscore the poem’s central tension.<|im_end|>

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and the speaker's emotional struggle:

1. **"Yet, do thy worst, old Time: despite thy wrong, / Whose fresh repair if now thou not renewest, / Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing:"**  
   (Themes: Time, beauty, unity)

2. **"So I, for fear of trust, forget to say / What acceptable audit canst thou leave?"**  
   (Themes: Trust, fear, legacy)

3. **"But not to tell of good or evil luck, / Within thine own bud buriest thy content"**  
   (Themes: Luck, hidden truths, beauty)

4. **"Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing: / Ah! if thou issueless shalt hap to die."**  
   (Themes: Death, unity, longing)

5. **"Unlook'd on diest, unless thou get a son. / With beauty's treasure, ere it be self-kill'd."**  
   (Themes: Death, beauty, self-preservation)

6. **"Which happies those that pay the willing loan; / Kissing with golden face the meadows green"**  
   (Themes: Love, beauty, reciprocity)

7. **"I make my love engrafted to this store: / Featured like him, like him with friends possess'd"**  
   (Themes: Love, beauty, companionship)

8. **"But not to tell of good or evil luck, / Whose speechless song, being many, seeming one"**  
   (Themes: Truth, unity, time)

9. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held, / So I, for fear of trust, forget to say"**  
   (Themes: Body, trust, legacy)

10. **"But wherefore do not you a mightier way / These poor rude lines of thy deceased lover?"**  
    (Themes: Love, mortality, poetic legacy)

11. **"And weep afresh love's long since cancell'd woe, / If thou couldst answer 'This fair child of mine'"**  
    (Themes: Loss, longing, love)

12. **"Resembling strong youth in his middle age, / Where wasteful Time debateth with Decay"**  
    (Themes: Time, beauty, aging)

13. **"A dearer birth than this his love had brought, / Mine be thy love and thy love's use their treasure"**  
    (Themes: Love, legacy, beauty)

14. **"So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night, / Or who is he so fond will be the tomb"**  
    (Themes: Love, beauty, self-deception)

15. **"Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts, / But I forbid thee one most heinous crime"**  
    (Themes: Love, restraint, self-awareness)

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of beauty, time, love, and the struggle against decay, along with their symbolic and emotional weight:

---

**1.**  
*And every fair from fair sometime declines,*  
*So I, made lame by fortune's dearest spite,*  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*  
*Compare them with the bettering of the time,*  
*When I do count the clock that tells the time,*  
*The bounteous largess given thee to give?*  

**2.**  
*When I do count the clock that tells the time,*  
*Like to the lark at break of day arising,*  
*Which happies those that pay the willing loan;*  
*Nature's bequest gives nothing but doth lend,*  
*And that fresh blood which youngly thou bestowest*  
*To hideous winter and confounds him there;*  

**3.**  
*And that unfair which fairly doth excel:*  
*Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,*  
*Pity the world, or else this glutton be,*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  
*Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?*  

**4.**  
*If thou survive my well-contented day,*  
*That I in thy abundance am sufficed*  
*With means more blessed than my barren rhyme?*  

**5.**  
*Then, were not summer's distillation left,*  
*But since he died and poets better prove,*  
*Mine eye hath play'd the painter and hath stell'd*  
*Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread*  
*But since she prick'd thee out for women's pleasure,*  
*Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone,*  
*Leese but their show; their substance still lives sweet.*  

**6.**  
*Making a famine where abundance lies,*  
*Their images I loved I view in thee,*  
*Then, were not summer's distillation left,*  
*And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*And many maiden gardens yet unset*  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy;*  

**7.**  
*That's for thyself to breed another thee,*  
*Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth.*  

**8.**  
*Which, used, lives th' executor to be.*  

**9.**  
*For to thy sensual fault I bring in sense--*  
*Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow,*  
*So then I am not lame, poor, nor despised,*  
*But since she prick'd thee out for women's pleasure,*  
*So thou through windows of thine age shall see*  
*Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,*  

**10.**  
*His tender heir might bear his memory:*  
*And in mine own love's strength seem to decay,*  
*As, thou being mine, mine is thy good report.*  

**11.**  
*Herein lives wisdom, beauty and increase:*  
*Stealing unseen to west with this disgrace:*  

**12.**  
*Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart,*  
*Bearing thy heart, which I will keep so chary*  
*And from the forlorn world his visage hide,*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  

**13.**  
*That thou consumest thyself in single life?*  
*Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date.*  

**14.**  
*Which husbandry in honour might uphold*  
*I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought,*  
*But since he died and poets better prove,*  
*She carved thee for her seal, and meant thereby*  
*That due of many now is thine alone:*  

**15.**  
*As an unperfect actor on the stage*  
*To the wide world and all her fading sweets;*  

**16.**  
*Their images I loved I view in thee,*  
*Then, were not summer's distillation left,*  
*And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*And many maiden gardens yet unset*  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy;*  

**17.**  
*That's for thyself to breed another thee,*  
*Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth.*  

---

These lines emphasize the tension between fleeting beauty and enduring love, the passage of time, and the poet's longing for connection. They reflect themes of impermanence, the struggle against decay, and the paradox of beauty being both a gift and a burden. Here are several lines from the poem that capture its emotional depth, themes of love, defiance against time, and the enduring legacy of the beloved:

1. **"She carved thee for her seal, and meant thereby  
To witness duty, not to show my wit:"**  
   *Themes: Love, duty, and the speaker's devotion.*

2. **"Make thee another self, for love of me,  
And in mine own love's strength seem to decay,  
And fortify yourself in your decay  
Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise."**  
   *Emotional contrast: The speaker's resolve to endure through time.*

3. **"The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife;  
As a decrepit father takes delight  
Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit."**  
   *Metaphors of loss and enduring love.*

4. **"Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion's paws,  
And from the forlorn world his visage hide,  
To say, within thine own deep-sunken eyes,  
From his low tract and look another way."**  
   *Defiance against time and the speaker's resolve.*

5. **"O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!"**  
   *A plea for the beloved to alter their perspective.*

6. **"My glass shall not persuade me I am old,  
And, tender churl, makest waste in niggarding."**  
   *Self-acceptance and the speaker's refusal to be defined by age.*

7. **"The dear repose for limbs with travel tired;  
When I consider every thing that grows  
Exceeded by the height of happier men."**  
   *The value of travel and the speaker's reflection on time.*

8. **"For beauty's pattern to succeeding men."**  
   *The enduring influence of beauty and love.*

9. **"Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,  
Nature's bequest gives nothing but doth lend,  
My body is the frame wherein 'tis held."**  
   *The speaker's devotion to the beloved's heart.*

10. **"Let those who are in favour with their stars  
Excusing thy sins more than thy sins are;  
No longer yours than you yourself here live:"**  
   *The speaker's assertion of their own worth.*

11. **"Ah! if thou issueless shalt hap to die.  
Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,  
So long as men can breathe or eyes can see,  
By unions married, do offend thine ear?"**  
   *The speaker's lament for the beloved's fate.*

12. **"Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?"**  
   *A poignant question about the speaker's sorrow.*

13. **"An eye more bright than theirs, less false in rolling,"**  
   *A metaphor for the speaker's desire for the beloved's gaze.*

14. **"The age to come would say 'This poet lies:  
Pointing to each his thunder, rain and wind,'**  
   *The speaker's defiance of time's judgment.*

15. **"In thy soul's thought, all naked, will bestow it;  
They draw but what they see, know not the heart."**  
   *The speaker's recognition of the beloved's inner truth.*

These lines encapsulate the poem's themes of love, time, defiance, and the enduring legacy of the beloved, while highlighting the speaker's emotional and philosophical struggles.<|im_end|>
<|endoftext|>How can I then return in happy plight,  
And puts apparel on my tatter'd loving,  
And do whate'er thou wilt, swift-footed Time,  
Save that my soul's imaginary sight  
Save breed, to brave him when he takes thee hence.  
To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way,  
That's for thyself to breed another thee,  
Flatter the mountain-tops with sovereign eye,  
Mark how one string, sweet husband to another,  
As, thou being mine, mine is thy good report.  
The age to come would say 'This poet lies:  
Pointing to each his thunder, rain and wind,'  
A dearer birth than this his love had brought,  
So should that beauty which you hold in lease  
Where all the treasure of thy lusty days,  
Leese but their show; their substance still lives sweet.  
But since she prick'd thee out for women's pleasure,  
To say, within thine own deep-sunken eyes,  
That use is not forbidden usury,  
Much liker than your painted counterfeit:  
That beauty still may live in thine or thee.  
The dear repose for limbs with travel tired;  
When I consider every thing that grows  
Exceeded by the height of happier men.  
And threescore year would make the world away.  
Much liker than your painted counterfeit:  
Despite of wrinkles this thy golden time.  
When forty winters shall beseige thy brow,  
And see thy blood warm when thou feel'st it cold.  
Then of thy beauty do I question make,  
Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit,  
Against the stormy gusts of winter's day  
Feed'st thy light'st flame with self-substantial fuel,  
But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:  
Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?  
This wish I have; then ten times happy me!  
FROM fairest creatures we desire increase,  
An eye more bright than theirs, less false in rolling,  
But as the marigold at the sun's eye,  
Without thy help by me be borne alone.  
To change your day of youth to sullied night;  
Within thine own bud buriest thy content  
So great a sum of sums, yet canst not live?  
That on himself such murderous shame commits.  
But that I hope some good conceit of thine  
Whilst that this shadow doth such substance give  
Despite of wrinkles this thy golden time.  
In thy soul's thought, all naked, will bestow it;  
They draw but what they see, know not the heart.  
But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:  
When day's oppression is not eased by night,  
Now see what good turns eyes for eyes have done:  
Which this, Time's pencil, or my pupil pen,  
Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date.  
Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue,  
Neither in inward worth nor outward fair,  
If it were fill'd with your most high deserts?  
In thy soul's thought, all naked, will bestow it;  
Now is the time that face should form another;  
Let me confess that we two must be twain,  
Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?  
Be, as thy presence is, gracious and kind,  
Entitled in thy parts do crowned sit,  
Or else receivest with pleasure thine annoy?  
And each, though enemies to either's reign,  
That beauty still may live in thine or thee.  
And though they be outstripp'd by every pen,  
In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, time, and the tension between ideal and reality:

---

**1.**  
*But thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*Nor it nor no remembrance what it was:*  
*Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,*  
*But wherefore do not you a mightier way*  
*For thou art so possess'd with murderous hate*  

**2.**  
*The bounteous largess given thee to give?*  
*The bounteous largess given thee to give?*  
*How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use,*  
*And moan the expense of many a vanish'd sight:*  

**3.**  
*So is it not with me as with that Muse*  
*Will play the tyrants to the very same*  
*But not to tell of good or evil luck,*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  

**4.**  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident:*  
*When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,*  
*Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart,*  
*Thou that art now the world's fresh ornament*  

**5.**  
*Myself corrupting, salving thy amiss,*  
*That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows*  
*And burn the long-lived phoenix in her blood:*  

**6.**  
*Lo! in the orient when the gracious light*  
*What acceptable audit canst thou leave?*  
*Where wasteful Time debateth with Decay,*  
*The painful warrior famoused for fight,*  
*Or ten times happier, be it ten for one:*  

**7.**  
*And though they be outstripp'd by every pen,*  
*Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,*  
*Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise.*  

**8.**  
*Thou art more lovely and more temperate:*  
*For at a frown they in their glory die.*  

**9.**  
*When forty winters shall beseige thy brow,*  
*Pity the world, or else this glutton be,*  
*From his low tract and look another way:*  
*When your sweet issue your sweet form should bear.*  

**10.**  
*O, that you were yourself! but, love, you are*  
*To give away yourself keeps yourself still,*  
*If thou survive my well-contented day,*  
*And many maiden gardens yet unset*  

**11.**  
*To dry the rain on my storm-beaten face,*  
*Who for thyself art so unprovident.*  

**12.**  
*But thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
*Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?*  

**13.**  
*So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night,*  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*Let those who are in favour with their stars*  
*Hiding thy bravery in their rotten smoke?*  

**14.**  
*Mine eyes have drawn thy shape, and thine for me*  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident:*  
*When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,*  
*Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart,*  
*Thou that art now the world's fresh ornament*  

**15.**  
*Although our undivided loves are one:*  
*Myself corrupting, salving thy amiss,*  
*Nor can I fortune to brief minutes tell,*  
*Those hours, that with gentle work did frame*  

---

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, transience, beauty, and the struggle between time and enduring beauty:

---

**1.**  
*The bounteous largess given thee to give?*  
*Yet, do thy worst, old Time: despite thy wrong,*  
*Then the conceit of this inconstant stay*  
*Anon permit the basest clouds to ride*  
*And trouble deal heaven with my bootless cries.*  

**2.**  
*If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:*

*The dear repose for limbs with travel tired,*

*Will be a tatter'd weed, of small worth held.*

*Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*

*But day doth daily draw my sorrows longer*

**5.**

*Beauty's effect with beauty were bereft,*  
*Disdains the tillage of thy husbandry?*

*When forty winters shall beseige thy brow,*

*Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate*  
*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*  
*That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:*  

**7.**  
*This wish I have; then ten times happy me!*  
*For beauty's pattern to succeeding men.*  

**8.**  
*Look, whom she best endow'd she gave the more;*  
*Now is the time that face should form another;*  
*Though thou repent, yet I have still the loss:*  
*Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing:*  
*How can I then be elder than thou art?*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  

**9.**  
*O, learn to read what silent love hath writ:*  
*Then, were not summer's distillation left,*  
*O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:*  
*For beauty's pattern to succeeding men.*  

**10.**  
*Such heavenly touches ne'er touch'd earthly faces.'*  
*Look, whom she best endow'd she gave the more;*  
*Now is the time that face should form another;*  
*Though thou repent, yet I have still the loss:*  
*Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing:*

*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*

Here are lines from the poem that capture its themes of eternal love, beauty, and the fleeting nature of time, along with their poetic significance:

---

**1.**  
*With ugly rack on his celestial face,*  
*In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:*  
*And though they be outstripp'd by every pen,*  
*O, that you were yourself! but, love, you are*  

**2.**  
*Who all in one, one pleasing note do sing:*  
*Holds in perfection but a little moment,*  
*Is from the book of honour razed quite,*  
*Shall hate be fairer lodged than gentle love?*  

**3.**  
*But things removed that hidden in thee lie!*  
*Points on me graciously with fair aspect*  
*Making a famine where abundance lies,*  
*Is it for fear to wet a widow's eye*  

**4.**  
*Nature's bequest gives nothing but doth lend,*  
*Sweets with sweets war not, joy delights in joy.*  

**5.**  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy;*  
*Then let not winter's ragged hand deface*  
*Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame,*  
*As I, not for myself, but for thee will:*  

**6.**  
*And all in war with Time for love of you,*  
*Save that my soul's imaginary sight*  
*So thou, thyself out-going in thy noon,*  
*To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir.*  

**7.**  
*Where all the treasure of thy lusty days,*  
*Leaving thee living in posterity?*  
*But thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*And your sweet semblance to some other give.*  

**8.**  
*Theirs for their style I'll read, his for his love.*  

**9.**  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*But things removed that hidden in thee lie!*  

**10.**  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*Then may I dare to boast how I do love thee:*  

**11.**  
*Hast thou, the master-mistress of my passion,*  
*Yourself again after yourself's decease,*  
*The sad account of fore-bemoaned moan,*  
*May make seem bare, in wanting words to show it:*  

**12.**  
*Suns of the world may stain when heaven's sun staineth.*  

**13.**  
*Roses have thorns, and silver fountains mud;*  
*Let those whom Nature hath not made for store,*  
*With what I most enjoy contented least,*  
*Wishing me like to one more rich in hope,*  
*Who heaven itself for ornament doth use.*  

**14.**  
*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*Which hides your life and shows not half your parts.*  

**15.**  
*Their images I loved I view in thee,*  
*But if the while I think on thee, dear friend,*  
*Let those whom Nature hath not made for store,*  
*Nor thou with public kindness honour me,*  
*Is it for fear to wet a widow's eye*  

**16.**  
*And yet methinks I have astronomy,*  
*Whose strength's abundance weakens his own heart.*  

**17.**  
*O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might.*  

**18.**  
*Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?*  

**19.**  
*Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age,*  
*Make thee another self, for love of me,*  
*Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate*  
*A man in hue, all 'hues' in his controlling,*  
*Which hides your life and shows not half your parts.*  

**20.**  
*Haply I think on thee, and then my state,*  
*So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night,*  
*Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind,*  
*Leaving thee living in posterity?*  

---

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its central themes of eternal love, the passage of time, and the speaker's devotion to the beloved:

1. **"But thy eternal summer shall not fade / Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame."**  
   *Eternal love and the defiance of time.*

2. **"Then can I grieve at grievances foregone, / Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone?"**  
   *The speaker’s lament for the beloved’s departure and the inevitability of time.*

3. **"The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife; / In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear."**  
   *The beloved’s absence and the speaker’s longing for their presence.*

4. **"O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!"**  
   *A plea for the beloved to alter their fate, reflecting the speaker’s devotion.*

5. **"My love shall in my verse ever live young."**  
   *Eternal love immortalized in the speaker’s poetry.*

6. **"If it were fill'd with your most high deserts? / Where I may not remove nor be removed."**  
   *The speaker’s hope for the beloved’s eternal favor and their own unchanging devotion.*

7. **"For precious friends hid in death's dateless night, / Or any of these all, or all, or more."**  
   *The speaker’s hope for the beloved’s enduring presence and the futility of worldly attachments.*

8. **"I may not evermore acknowledge thee, / Yourself again after yourself's decease."**  
   *The speaker’s longing for the beloved’s eternal beauty and their own unchanging love.*

9. **"She carved thee for her seal, and meant thereby / 'Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age.'"**  
   *The beloved’s creation and the speaker’s hope for their eternal beauty.*

10. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held, / No love toward others in that bosom sits."**  
    *The speaker’s devotion to the beloved, rejecting worldly attachments.*

11. **"If thou couldst answer 'This fair child of mine / If I could write the beauty of your eyes.'"**  
    *The speaker’s desire to capture the beloved’s beauty in poetry.*

12. **"O, learn to read what silent love hath writ: / And in fresh numbers number all your graces."**  
    *The speaker’s hope to understand and immortalize the beloved’s virtues.*

13. **"Ten times thyself were happier than thou art, / Points on me graciously with fair aspect."**  
    *The speaker’s admiration for the beloved’s beauty and their own longing.*

14. **"My love shall in my verse ever live young."**  
    *Eternal love immortalized in the speaker’s poetry.*

These lines encapsulate the poem’s themes of eternal love, the speaker’s devotion, the struggle against time, and the enduring beauty of the beloved. They reflect the poem’s central conflict between the impermanence of time and the immortality of love.<|im_end|>

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, self-reflection, and the tension between self-love and others' perceptions:

1. **"And look upon myself and curse my fate, / What acceptable audit canst thou leave?"**  
2. **"Feed'st thy light'st flame with self-substantial fuel, / So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night."**  
3. **"For having traffic with thyself alone, / Sets you most rich in youth before my sight, / And in mine own love's strength seem to decay."**  
4. **"The world will be thy widow and still weep / The offender's sorrow lends but weak relief."**  
5. **"If it were fill'd with your most high deserts? / Then of thy beauty do I question make."**  
6. **"For having traffic with thyself alone, / So I, for fear of trust, forget to say."**  
7. **"So then I am not lame, poor, nor despised, / That's for thyself to breed another thee."**  
8. **"A dearer birth than this his love had brought, / Are windows to my breast, where-through the sun / That thou no form of thee hast left behind, / By chance or nature's changing course untrimm'd."**  
9. **"But when in thee time's furrows I behold, / As an unperfect actor on the stage / And summer's lease hath all too short a date."**  
10. **"For having traffic with thyself alone, / So I, for fear of trust, forget to say."**  
11. **"To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir."**  
12. **"For shame! deny that thou bear'st love to any, / Mine eyes have drawn thy shape, and thine for me."**  
13. **"But when in thee time's furrows I behold, / That then I scorn to change my state with kings."**  
14. **"The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife; / Then let not winter's ragged hand deface / That thou consumest thyself in single life?"**  
15. **"For having traffic with thyself alone, / So shall those blots that do with me remain."**  
16. **"A woman's gentle heart, but not acquainted / Mine eyes have drawn thy shape, and thine for me."**  
17. **"But day doth daily draw my sorrows longer / That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes."**  
18. **"Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind, / A dearer birth than this his love had brought."**  
19. **"To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir."**  
20. **"And every fair from fair sometime declines, / And summer's lease hath all too short a date."**  

These lines emphasize the speaker's internal conflict, the beauty of the beloved, and the paradox of self-love versus external perception. The poem's themes of self-reflection, love, and the tension between individuality and connection are vividly captured here.<|im_end|>

Ah! if thou issueless shalt hap to die.
In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:
And dumb presagers of my speaking breast,
To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way,
Featured like him, like him with friends possess'd,
But if thou live, remember'd not to be,
The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife;
Thou of thyself thy sweet self dost deceive.
And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:
Find no determination: then you were
That heaven's air in this huge rondure hems.
Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone,
Which though it alter not love's sole effect,
As he takes from you, I engraft you new.
Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,
But if thou live, remember'd not to be,
So I, made lame by fortune's dearest spite,
Which erst from heat did canopy the herd,
So long lives this and this gives life to thee.

Die single, and thine image dies with thee.

Such civil war is in my love and hate

Desiring this man's art and that man's scope,
Who lets so fair a house fall to decay,
The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife;
When forty winters shall beseige thy brow,
O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary
And all the rest forgot for which he toil'd:
For all that beauty that doth cover thee
When every private widow well may keep
That thou among the wastes of time must go,
For whether beauty, birth, or wealth, or wit,
Feed'st thy light'st flame with self-substantial fuel,
Whose speechless song, being many, seeming one,
As any mother's child, though not so bright
Where wasteful Time debateth with Decay,
To work my mind, when body's work's expired:
Whilst I, whom fortune of such triumph bars,
How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use,
When day's oppression is not eased by night,
For thou art so possess'd with murderous hate
To hear with eyes belongs to love's fine wit.
And only herald to the gaudy spring,
That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.
But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:
As any mother's child, though not so bright
For then my thoughts, from far where I abide,
And do whate'er thou wilt, swift-footed Time,
By unions married, do offend thine ear,
By chance or nature's changing course untrimm'd;
For thee and for myself no quiet find.

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and longing:

1. **"And do whate'er thou wilt, swift-footed Time, / And barren rage of death's eternal cold?"**  
2. **"These poor rude lines of thy deceased lover, / Whose speechless song, being many, seeming one, / The perfect ceremony of love's rite, / To thee I send this written embassage, / With all triumphant splendor on my brow;"**  
3. **"For all that beauty that doth cover thee / And sable curls all silver'd o'er with white; / Kissing with golden face the meadows green, / No love toward others in that bosom sits;"**  
4. **"O' let me, true in love, but truly write, / Yet doth it steal sweet hours from love's delight."**  
5. **"And that fresh blood which youngly thou bestowest / As truth and beauty shall together thrive, / Which to repair should be thy chief desire."**  
6. **"Thy unused beauty must be tomb'd with thee, / Excusing thy sins more than thy sins are; / For all that beauty that doth cover thee / And sable curls all silver'd o'er with white;"**  
7. **"The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife; / Whose speechless song, being many, seeming one, / To hideous winter and confounds him there;"**  
8. **"Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising, / Stirr'd by a painted beauty to his verse, / For thou art so possess'd with murderous hate / But things removed that hidden in thee lie!"**  
9. **"Like to the lark at break of day arising / Resembling sire and child and happy mother / Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?"**  
10. **"And do whate'er thou wilt, swift-footed Time, / For having traffic with thyself alone, / And from the forlorn world his visage hide, / That thou consumest thyself in single life?"**  
11. **"Where all the treasure of thy lusty days, / And dumb presagers of my speaking breast, / Presents thy shadow to my sightless view, / A liquid prisoner pent in walls of glass;"**  
12. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held, / And there reigns love and all love's loving parts, / As those gold candles fix'd in heaven's air."**  
13. **"Let me confess that we two must be twain, / To hideous winter and confounds him there; / Yet, do thy worst, old Time: despite thy wrong, / Or ten times happier, be it ten for one;"**  
14. **"And all those friends which I thought buried. / When in eternal lines to time thou growest, / As interest of the dead, which now appear."**  
15. **"Shall sum my count and make my old excuse, / Profitless usurer, why dost thou use / Neither in inward worth nor outward fair, / Save that my soul's imaginary sight."**  
16. **"And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill. / Which to repair should be thy chief desire."**  
17. **"The sun and moon, with earth and sea's rich gems, / Be not self-will'd, for thou art much too fair."**  
18. **"As an unperfect actor on the stage, / So should my papers yellow'd with their age."**  
19. **"And each, though enemies to either's reign, / Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May, / Now is the time that face should form another."**  

These lines emphasize the speaker's love, the fleeting nature of time, the beauty of the deceased lover, and the struggle between passion and mortality. The poem blends poetic imagery with emotional resonance, capturing the essence of loss and enduring love.<|im_end|>

---

**1.**  
*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings*  
*Calls back the lovely April of her prime:*  
*Is from the book of honour razed quite,*  
*But when in thee time's furrows I behold,*  
*And night doth nightly make grief's strength seem stronger.*  

---

**2.**  
*In thy soul's thought, all naked, will bestow it;*  
*Nor lose possession of that fair thou owest;*  
*Unless thou take that honour from thy name:*  
*That am debarr'd the benefit of rest?*  

---

**3.**  
*Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,*  
*How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use,*  
*But when in thee time's furrows I behold,*  
*Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?*  

---

**4.**  
*Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?*  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*That's for thyself to breed another thee,*  
*That on himself such murderous shame commits.*

**5.**

*Where I may not remove nor be removed.*

**6.**  
*Thou art thy mother's glass, and she in thee,*  
*After a thousand victories once foil'd,*  
*Nor lose possession of that fair thou owest;*  
*And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
*That beauty still may live in thine or thee.*  

---

**7.**  
*The one by toil, the other to complain,*  
*Where I may not remove nor be removed.*  
*And moan the expense of many a vanish'd sight:*  
*Which to repair should be thy chief desire.*  

---

*Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel,*  
*Serving with looks his sacred majesty,*  
*Since sweets and beauties do themselves forsake,*

---

*For where is she so fair whose unear'd womb*  
*And often is his gold complexion dimm'd;*  
*Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel.*  
*So is it not with me as with that Muse*  
*Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most.*

   *Calls back the lovely April of her prime:*  
   *Is from the book of honour razed quite,*  
   *But when in thee time's furrows I behold,*  
   *And night doth nightly make grief's strength seem stronger.*

2. *In thy soul's thought, all naked, will bestow it;*  
   *Nor lose possession of that fair thou owest;*  
   *Unless thou take that honour from thy name:*  
   *That am debarr'd the benefit of rest?*

3. *Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,*

   *But when in thee time's furrows I behold,*  
   *Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?*

5. *O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:*  
   *Where I may not remove nor be removed.*

6. *Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel,*  
   *Serving with looks his sacred majesty,*  
   *Since sweets and beauties do themselves forsake,*  
   *And every fair from fair sometime declines.*

7. *O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!*  
   *Since sweets and beauties do themselves forsake,*  
   *Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
   *In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:*  
   *My glass shall not persuade me I am old.*

8. *For where is she so fair whose unear'd womb*  
   *And often is his gold complexion dimm'd;*  
   *Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel.*  
   *So is it not with me as with that Muse*  
   *Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most.*

---

**1.**  
*To say, within thine own deep-sunken eyes,*

*Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,*

**5.**

*Nor draw no lines there with thine antique pen;*  
*O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:*

*And dumb presagers of my speaking breast,*  
*As any mother's child, though not so bright*  
*And every fair from fair sometime declines,*  
*Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard,*  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase,*  
*By chance or nature's changing course untrimm'd;*  

**14.**  
*Which, like a jewel hung in ghastly night,*  
*When sparkling stars twire not thou gild'st the even.*  

**15.**  
*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*

*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*  
*Excusing thy sins more than thy sins are;*  

**23.**

**25.**  
*Much steals men's eyes and women's souls amazeth.*  

**26.**  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*

*And see thy blood warm when thou feel'st it cold.*

---

**1.**  
*That then I scorn to change my state with kings.*  
*(Themes: defiance of authority, steadfast love, and the speaker’s refusal to be dictated by power.)*

**2.**  
*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*  
*Thy adverse party is thy advocate.*  
*(Themes: self-perception vs. reality, the beloved’s role as a guide, and the speaker’s contentment.)*

**3.**  
*Feed’st thy light’st flame with self-substantial fuel,*  
*But if thou live, remember’d not to be.*  
*(Themes: sustaining love through selflessness, the transient nature of life, and the speaker’s hope for the beloved’s memory.)*

**4.**  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy,*  
*If thou survive my well-contented day.*  
*(Themes: the speaker’s longing for the beloved’s presence, even in death, and the transformative power of love.)*

**5.**  
*The eyes, 'fore duteous, now converted are,*  
*For having traffic with thyself alone.*  
*(Themes: transformation of the beloved’s gaze, the speaker’s longing for their presence, and the duality of love and self.)*

**6.**  
*So shall those blots that do with me remain*  
*Thy youth's proud livery, so gazed on now.*  
*(Themes: the permanence of love’s impact, the speaker’s acceptance of the beloved’s beauty, and the enduring mark of affection.)*

**7.**  
*For through the painter must you see his skill,*  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase.*  
*(Themes: the impermanence of beauty, the speaker’s awareness of time’s passage, and the inevitability of change.)*

**8.**  
*But not to tell of good or evil luck,*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  
*(Themes: the speaker’s refusal to commodify love, the beloved’s influence on the speaker’s perception, and the sacredness of their connection.)*

**9.**  
*If thou live, remember’d not to be,*  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy.*  
*(Themes: the speaker’s hope for the beloved’s memory, the transformative power of love, and the duality of beauty and decay.)*

**10.**  
*Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,*  
*I may not evermore acknowledge thee,*  
*(Themes: the inevitability of death, the speaker’s longing for the beloved’s presence, and the paradox of love’s endurance.)*

**11.**  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*  
*Now see what good turns eyes for eyes have done.*  
*(Themes: the speaker’s acceptance of time’s passage, the transformative power of love, and the duality of loss and renewal.)*

**12.**  
*The eyes, 'fore duteous, now converted are,*  
*For having traffic with thyself alone.*  
*(Themes: the transformation of the beloved’s gaze, the speaker’s longing for their presence, and the duality of love and self.)*

**13.**  
*So should the lines of life that life repair,*  
*The one by toil, the other to complain.*  
*(Themes: the speaker’s acceptance of life’s impermanence, the duality of struggle and joy, and the enduring nature of love.)*

**14.**  
*And weep afresh love's long since cancell'd woe,*  
*In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear.*  
*(Themes: the speaker’s grief for lost love, the duality of love’s demands, and the paradox of selflessness in affection.)*

**15.**  
*For through the painter must you see his skill,*  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase.*  
*(Themes: the impermanence of beauty, the speaker’s awareness of time’s passage, and the transformative power of love.)*

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, time, and the tension between duty and longing:

1. **"In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear."**  
2. **"All men make faults, and even I in this, Look, whom she best endow'd she gave the more;"**  
3. **"Lo! in the orient when the gracious light Kissing with golden face the meadows green."**  
4. **"The eyes, 'fore duteous, now converted are; When in eternal lines to time thou growest: Harsh featureless and rude, barrenly perish:"**  
5. **"Roses have thorns, and silver fountains mud; Thy youth's proud livery, so gazed on now, The perfect ceremony of love's rite:"**  
6. **"Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts, If ten of thine ten times refigured thee: Proving his beauty by succession thine!"**  
7. **"O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought: O, that you were yourself! but, love, you are;"**  
8. **"The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell, For beauty's pattern to succeeding men."**  
9. **"For whether beauty, birth, or..."**  
10. **"The perfect ceremony of love's rite, For thou art so possess'd with murderous hate A man in hue, all 'hues' in his controlling, As those gold candles fix'd in heaven's air:"**  
11. **"Yet him for this my love no whit disdaineth; Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts, If ten of thine ten times refigured thee: Proving his beauty by succession thine!"**  
12. **"I all alone beweep my outcast state To march in ranks of better equipage: Even so my sun one early morn did shine Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?"**  
13. **"Let them say more than like of hearsay well; Makes black night beauteous and her old face new."**  
14. **"The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now. Are windows to my breast, where-through the sun Born on the bier with white and bristly beard, And weep afresh love's long since cancell'd woe, That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes."**  
15. **"So long as youth and thou are of one date; Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate She carved thee for her seal, and meant thereby For to thy sensual fault I bring in sense-- Look, whom she best endow'd she gave the more;"**  

These lines emphasize themes of love, beauty, time, and the inevitability of loss, with vivid imagery and emotional depth. The poem's tone is both melancholic and poetic, reflecting the struggle between duty and desire.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, time, and eternal love, while preserving the poetic structure and imagery:

---

**1.**  
*Yet eyes this cunning want to grace their art;*  
*And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:*  
*Mine be thy love and thy love's use their treasure.*  
*Such heavenly touches ne'er touch'd earthly faces.'*  

**2.**  
*So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night,*  
*Such heavenly touches ne'er touch'd earthly faces.'*  

**3.**  
*And look upon myself and curse my fate,*  
*But when in thee time's furrows I behold,*  
*And only herald to the gaudy spring,*  
*Herein lives wisdom, beauty and increase:*  

**4.**  
*When in eternal lines to time thou growest:*  
*That heaven's air in this huge rondure hems.*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  

**5.**  
*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings*  
*Beauty's effect with beauty were bereft,*  
*Whose fresh repair if now thou not renewest,*  
*Mine eye hath play'd the painter and hath stell'd*  

**6.**  
*Of public honour and proud titles boast,*  
*Such civil war is in my love and hate*  
*Yet him for this my love no whit disdaineth;*  
*And there reigns love and all love's loving parts,*  

**7.**  
*Excusing thy sins more than thy sins are;*  
*Where I may not remove nor be removed.*  

**8.**  
*So shall those blots that do with me remain*  
*Against the stormy gusts of winter's day*  
*Which erst from heat did canopy the herd,*  
*Delights to peep, to gaze therein on thee;*  

**9.**  
*Who heaven itself for ornament doth use*  
*My love shall in my verse ever live young.*  

**10.**  
*Shall hate be fairer lodged than gentle love?*  
*I make my love engrafted to this store:*  

**11.**  
*But day doth daily draw my sorrows longer*  
*With shifting change, as is false women's fashion;*  

**12.**  
*And often is his gold complexion dimm'd;*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  

**13.**  
*Be not self-will'd, for thou art much too fair*  
*No love toward others in that bosom sits*  
*And dost him grace when clouds do blot the heaven:*  

**14.**  
*And all those friends which I thought buried.*  

**15.**  
*Like feeble age, he reeleth from the day,*  
*But if thou live, remember'd not to be,*  

**16.**  
*Where all the treasure of thy lusty days,*  
*Now see what good turns eyes for eyes have done:*  

**17.**  
*For all that beauty that doth cover thee*  
*Mark how one string, sweet husband to another,*  

**18.**  
*Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee:*  

**19.**  
*From sullen earth, sings hymns at heaven's gate;*  
*Even so my sun one early morn did shine*  

**20.**  
*The painful warrior famoused for fight,*  
*Weary with toil, I haste me to my bed,*  

**21.**  
*Which bounteous gift thou shouldst in bounty cherish:*  

**22.**  
*As truth and beauty shall together thrive,*  
*That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:*  

**23.**  
*But since he died and poets better prove,*  
*O, learn to read what silent love hath writ:*  

**24.**  
*That thereby beauty's rose might never die,*  

**25.**  
*With what I most enjoy contented least;*  
*Cheered and cheque'd even by the self-same sky,*  

**26.**  
*When sparkling stars twire not thou gild'st the even.*  

**27.**  
*They do but sweetly chide thee, who confounds*  
*Duty so great, which wit so poor as mine*  
*That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:*  

**28.**  
*Or else of thee this I prognosticate:*  
*I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought,*  

**29.**  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*Shall hate be fairer lodged than gentle love?*  

**30.**  
*And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill.*  

---

These lines emphasize the poem's themes of eternal love, the contrast between beauty and time, and the enduring nature of love despite life's challenges. The repeated motifs of "heavenly touches," "eternal lines," and "love's enduring parts" are central to the poem's message.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and the impermanence of life, along with key imagery and emotional resonance:

---

**1.**  
*And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste,*  
*Then can I grieve at grievances foregone,*  
*By oft predict that I in heaven find:*  
*Till Nature, as she wrought thee, fell a-doting,*  
*Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel.*  

**2.**  
*So I, for fear of trust, forget to say,*  
*O, let my books be then the eloquence*  
*Lo! in the orient when the gracious light*  
*And in fresh numbers number all your graces,*  
*Pointing to each his thunder, rain and wind,*  
*But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:*  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings*  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
*Which in my bosom's shop is hanging still,*  
*Or any of these all, or all, or more,*  
*And trouble deal heaven with my bootless cries*  
*And loathsome canker lives in sweetest bud.*  

**3.**  
*But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,*  
*Doth homage to his new-appearing sight,*  
*Let me confess that we two must be twain,*  
*And then believe me, my love is as fair*  
*But wherefore do not you a mightier way*  
*As a decrepit father takes delight*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  
*And barren rage of death's eternal cold?*  

**4.**  
*An eye more bright than theirs, less false in rolling,*  
*But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,*  
*Such heavenly touches ne'er touch'd earthly faces.'*  

**5.**  
*When lofty trees I see barren of leaves*  
*And barren rage of death's eternal cold?*  
*And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  
*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*Make war upon this bloody tyrant, Time?*  

**6.**  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*But wherefore do not you a mightier way*  
*Stirr'd by a painted beauty to his verse,*  
*Thou shouldst print more, not let that copy die.*  

**7.**  
*With what I most enjoy contented least;*  
*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*  
*And burn the long-lived phoenix in her blood;*  
*That I an accessary needs must be*  
*As an unperfect actor on the stage*  
*And puts apparel on my tatter'd loving,*  
*With April's first-born flowers, and all things rare*  
*Makes black night beauteous and her old face new.*  

**8.**  
*Him in thy course untainted do allow*  
*Mine eyes have drawn thy shape, and thine for me*  
*Ah! but those tears are pearl which thy love sheds,*  
*Leese but their show; their substance still lives sweet.*  

**9.**  
*Which this, Time's pencil, or my pupil pen,*  
*To show me worthy of thy sweet respect:*  
*But things removed that hidden in thee lie!*  
*Thou art more lovely and more temperate:*  
*From his low tract and look another way:*  
*But wherefore do not you a mightier way*  
*Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy;*  
*Hast thou, the master-mistress of my passion;*  
*Die single, and thine image dies with thee.*  

**10.**  
*By unions married, do offend thine ear,*  
*When your sweet issue your sweet form should bear.*  
*When, in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes,*  
*For thy sweet love remember'd such wealth brings*  
*I tell the day, to please them thou art bright*  
*By adding one thing to my purpose nothing.*  
*With virtuous wish would bear your living flowers,*  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
*But thou, contracted to thine own bright eyes,*  
*As, thou being mine, mine is thy good report.*  

**11.**  
*Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread*  
*So shall those blots that do with me remain*  
*Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate*  
*Be not self-will'd, for thou art much too fair*  
*And dig deep trenches in thy beauty's field,*  
*Like feeble age, he reeleth from the day,*  
*Then being ask'd where all thy beauty lies,*  
*So long lives this and this gives life to thee.*  

**12.**  
*Yet mortal looks adore his beauty still,*  
*And dumb presagers of my speaking breast,*  
*And dumb presagers of my speaking breast,*  
*And yet methinks I have astronomy,*  
*Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother.*  

**13.**  
*With April's first-born flowers, and all things rare*  
*Will be a tatter'd weed, of small worth held:*  
*Is from the book of honour razed quite,*  
*Without thy help by me be borne alone.*  

**14.**  
*My love shall in my verse ever live young.*  

**15.**  
*Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,*  
*And in fresh numbers number all your graces,*  
*And thou, all they, hast all the all of me.*  

**16.**  
*Thy youth's proud livery, so gazed on now,*  
*Within thine own bud buriest thy content*  
*When day's oppression is not eased by night,*  
*O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!*  

**17.**  
*Resembling sire and child and happy mother*  
*And by addition me of thee defeated,*  
*Let those who are in favour with their stars*  
*Ah! but those tears are pearl which thy love sheds,*  
*When day's oppression is not eased by night,*  
*And every fair from fair sometime declines,*  
*But out, alack! he was but one hour mine;*  
*Anon permit the basest clouds to ride*  
*

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, selflessness, and the enduring nature of beauty, along with key motifs and emotional depth:

1. **"For having traffic with thyself alone,  
   Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion's paws,"**  
   *(Themes: selflessness, time, and the power of love to defy time.)*

2. **"Then can I grieve at grievances foregone,  
   And night doth nightly make grief's strength seem stronger."**  
   *(Emotional resilience and the paradox of grief.)*

3. **"Unthrifty loveliness, why dost thou spend  
   So great a sum of sums, yet canst not live?"**  
   *(Critique of love’s cost and the poet’s selfless devotion.)*

4. **"Mine eyes have drawn thy shape, and thine for me  
   For shame! deny that thou bear'st love to any,"**  
   *(Direct address to the beloved, highlighting the poet’s love.)*

5. **"Then happy I, that love and am beloved  
   For having traffic with thyself alone,"**  
   *(The poet’s acceptance of selfless love.)*

6. **"O, let my books be then the eloquence  
   And all those friends which I thought buried."**  
   *(The poet’s desire to preserve the beloved’s memory.)*

7. **"To give away yourself keeps yourself still,  
   That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace."**  
   *(Selflessness as a healing act.)*

8. **"Resembling strong youth in his middle age,  
   So long as youth and thou are of one date."**  
   *(The eternal nature of love.)*

9. **"Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow,  
   O, let my books be then the eloquence..."**  
   *(The poet’s longing to preserve the beloved’s beauty.)*

10. **"The world will be thy widow and still weep  
    Him in thy course untainted do allow."**  
    *(The poet’s desire to protect the beloved’s purity.)*

11. **"For never-resting time leads summer on  
    You should live twice; in it and in my rhyme."**  
    *(The paradox of time and the poet’s enduring love.)*

12. **"The dear repose for limbs with travel tired,  
    The eyes, 'fore duteous, now converted are."**  
    *(The poet’s longing for the beloved’s presence.)*

13. **"I will not praise that purpose not to sell.  
    And see the brave day sunk in hideous night."**  
    *(The poet’s rejection of superficial praise.)*

14. **"Thy youth's proud livery, so gazed on now,  
    Sets you most rich in youth before my sight."**  
    *(The beloved’s beauty and the poet’s admiration.)*

15. **"I all alone beweep my outcast state  
    Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone."**  
    *(The poet’s sorrow for the beloved’s impermanence.)*

16. **"Mine eye hath play'd the painter and hath stell'd  
    That thou no form of thee hast left behind."**  
    *(The poet’s longing to preserve the beloved’s memory.)*

17. **"And that fresh blood which youngly thou bestowest  
    And see the brave day sunk in hideous night."**  
    *(The poet’s desire to protect the beloved from time’s decay.)*

18. **"Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date."**  
    *(The poet’s acceptance of the beloved’s fate.)*

19. **"But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,  
    Making a couplement of proud compare."**  
    *(The enduring power of beauty.)*

20. **"O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary  
    How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use."**  
    *(The poet’s plea for love to be selfless.)*

These lines highlight the poem’s themes of love, selflessness, and the paradox of beauty enduring despite time. The poet’s voice is both tender and profound, emphasizing the depth of their affection and the enduring nature of their bond.<|im_end|>

   *A comparison of the beloved’s beauty to others, emphasizing their uniqueness.*  

7. **"And keep my drooping eyelids open wide, / You had a father: let your son say so."**  
   *A poignant line about the speaker’s own mortality and the beloved’s influence.*  

8. **"Leese but their show; their substance still lives sweet."**  
   *A critique of superficiality, emphasizing the enduring value of the beloved’s true essence.*  

9. **"Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone, / O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might."**  
   *A metaphor for the speaker’s love as a heavy, enduring burden.*  

10. **"Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother."**  
    *A critique of the beloved’s ability to deceive and captivate.*  

11. **"And in one of thine, from that which thou departest, / But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:"**  
    *A warning against the speaker’s own transgressions, reflecting on love’s complexity.*  

12. **"If I could write the beauty of your eyes / Hath dear religious love stol'n from mine eye."**  
    *A poignant line about the loss of love and the speaker’s longing.*  

13. **"Their images I loved I view in thee, / From his low tract and look another way:"**  
    *A reflection on the speaker’s deep, unrequited love.*  

14. **"By children's eyes her husband's shape in mind."**  
    *A metaphor for the beloved’s enduring presence in the speaker’s memory.*  

15. **"I may not evermore acknowledge thee, / Exceeded by the height of happier men."**  
    *A declaration of the beloved’s unparalleled beauty and the speaker’s longing.*  

16. **"In one of thine, from that which thou departest, / And summer's green all girded up in sheaves."**  
    *A vivid image of the beloved’s enduring presence and the speaker’s resolve to cherish them.*  

17. **"O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow, / The painful warrior famoused for fight."**  
    *A plea to not be consumed by the speaker’s own grief, emphasizing the beloved’s worth.*  

18. **"If ten of thine ten times refigured thee, / No love toward others in that bosom sits."**  
    *A paradox of the beloved’s beauty: it elevates the speaker but also isolates them.*  

19. **"To find where your true image pictured lies, / Ah! but those tears are pearl which thy love sheds."**  
    *A reflection on the speaker’s own vulnerability and the beloved’s enduring impact.*  

20. **"If all were minded so, the times should cease, / Then look I death my days should expiate."**  
    *A plea to cherish the beloved before time ends, emphasizing the fleeting nature of life.*
