Here are the key lines from the poem, selected for their thematic depth, repetition, and emotional resonance:

1. **"As he takes from you, I engraft you new."**  
2. **"The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell,"**  
3. **"They do but sweetly chide thee, who confounds."**  
4. **"If thou survive my well-contented day, Will be a tatter'd weed, of small worth held:"**  
5. **"That I in thy abundance am sufficed."**  
6. **"Profitless usurer, why dost thou use?"**  
7. **"Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread, Attending on his golden pilgrimage;"**  
8. **"Against the stormy gusts of winter's day, Hiding thy bravery in their rotten smoke?"**  
9. **"For thou art so possess'd with murderous hate, Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate."**  
10. **"O, none but unthrifts! Dear my love, you know."**  
11. **"And all those friends which I thought buried."**  
12. **"Resembling sire and child and happy mother, Thou that art now the world's fresh ornament."**  
13. **"Make glad and sorry seasons as thou fleets, How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use?"**  
14. **"Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth."**  
15. **"If the true concord of well-tuned sounds, Neither in inward worth nor outward fair, But were some child of yours alive that time, Yet, do thy worst, old Time: despite thy wrong, Looking on darkness which the blind do see, Profitless usurer, why dost thou use?"**  
16. **"And summer's lease hath all too short a date: As he takes from you, I engraft you new."**  
17. **"But when from highmost pitch, with weary car, Profitless usurer, why dost thou use?"**  
18. **"Where all the treasure of thy lusty days, Till Nature, as she wrought thee, fell a-doting, Against this coming end you should prepare, With what I most enjoy contented least;"**  
19. **"Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?"**  
20. **"For all that beauty that doth cover thee, Then may I dare to boast how I do love thee; Thou art more lovely and more temperate:"**  
21. **"But day by day, and night by day, oppress'd?"**  
22. **"yet mortal looks adore his beauty still, That's for thyself to breed another thee, Exceeded by the height of happier men."**  
23. **"To show me worthy of thy sweet respect: Strikes each in each by mutual ordering, Of public honour and proud titles boast, And they are rich and ransom all ill deeds."**  
24. **"That am debarr'd the benefit of rest? Then being ask'd where all thy beauty lies, Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone, The perfect ceremony of love's rite, Clouds and eclipses stain both moon and sun, And trouble deal heaven with my bootless cries, Thou gavest me thine, not to give back again."**  
25. **"And stretched metre of an antique song: Ah! if thou issueless shalt hap to die."**  
26. **"And thou, all they, hast all the all of me."**  
27. **"And dumb presagers of my speaking breast, Till whatsoever star that guides my moving, When every private widow well may keep, Sings hymns at heaven's gate; Nor lose possession of that fair thou owest; Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone, Doth homage to his new-appearing sight, Strikes each in each by mutual ordering, Full many a glorious morning have I seen, So is it not with me as with that Muse."**  
28. **"O' let me, true in love, but truly write, The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now."**  
29. **"Nor can I fortune to brief minutes tell, Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard, Then being ask'd where all thy beauty lies, Compare them with the bettering of the time, More than that tongue that more hath more express'd."**  
30. **"Compare them with the bettering of the time, Then look I death my days should expiate."**  
31. **"But if the while I think on thee, dear friend, And many maiden gardens yet unset, Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel."**  
32. **"The world will wail thee, like a makeless wife; 'Tis not enough that through the cloud thou break, If the true concord of well-tuned sounds, As tender nurse her babe from faring ill."**  
33. **"So then I am not lame, poor, nor despised, But if the while I think on thee, dear friend, And that unfair which fairly doth excel: Pointing to each his thunder, rain and wind, Or who is he so fond will be the tomb."**  
34. **"So long as youth and thou are of one date; Then of thy beauty do I question make, Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread, Look, what an unthrift in the world doth spend, Bearing thy heart, which I will keep so chary, Beauty o'ersnow'd and bareness every where: Pluck the keen teeth from the fierce tiger's jaws, And though they

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, duty, and the transience of life, along with their emotional weight and poetic devices:

---

**1.**  
*Wishing me like to one more rich in hope,*  
*To witness duty, not to show my wit:*  
*When lofty trees I see barren of leaves*  
*Will play the tyrants to the very same.*  

**2.**  
*If I could write the beauty of your eyes*  
*Hiding thy bravery in their rotten smoke?*  

**3.**  
*Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother.*  
*But flowers distill'd though they with winter meet,*  
*And, constant stars, in them I read such art.*  

**4.**  
*For whether beauty, birth, or wealth, or wit,*  
*Weary with toil, I haste me to my bed,*  
*And perspective it is the painter's art.*  

**5.**  
*Mark how one string, sweet husband to another,*  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live,*  
*Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date.*  

**6.**  
*O, let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*If I could write the beauty of your eyes*  
*Hiding thy bravery in their rotten smoke?*  

**7.**  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary*  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live.*  

**8.**  
*And every fair from fair sometime declines,*  
*Which, like a jewel hung in ghastly night,*  
*By unions married, do offend thine ear.*  

**9.**  
*To that sweet thief which sourly robs from me.*  
*Who heaven itself for ornament doth use*  
*And see the brave day sunk in hideous night.*  

**10.**  
*The painful warrior famoused for fight,*  
*The sad account of fore-bemoaned moan,*  
*Since sweets and beauties do themselves forsake*  
*But not to tell of good or evil luck.*  

**11.**  
*O, let my books be then the eloquence*  
*Hath dear religious love stol'n from mine eye*  
*And nothing 'gainst Time's scythe can make defence.*  

**12.**  
*Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,*  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident.*  

**13.**  
*And your true rights be term'd a poet's rage,*  
*With means more blessed than my barren rhyme?*  

**14.**  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary*  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live.*  

**15.**  
*And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
*As interest of the dead, which now appear.*  

---

These lines emphasize themes of love, beauty, duty, and the fleeting nature of life, using metaphors like "the grave where buried love doth live" and "beauty's effect with beauty were bereft." They reflect the poem's tension between idealized beauty and the harsh realities of existence, as well as the speaker's struggle to maintain love in a world of impermanence.<|im_end|>

Here are several lines from the poem that encapsulate its themes of love, time, beauty, and the speaker's emotional struggle:

1. **"Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse / Nor draw no lines there with thine antique pen;"**  
2. **"The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell, / The age to come would say 'This poet lies:'**  
3. **"So is it not with me as with that Muse / Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard;"**  
4. **"But I forbid thee one most heinous crime: / Make thee another self, for love of me;"**  
5. **"To dry the rain on my storm-beaten face, / To dry the rain on my storm-beaten face;"**  
6. **"O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!"**  
7. **"The stars in secret influence comment; / If thou survive my well-contented day, / Thou mayst call thine when thou from youth convertest."**  
8. **"The world will be thy widow and still weep, / Exceeded by the height of happier men."**  
9. **"Nature's bequest gives nothing but doth lend, / Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee:"**  
10. **"And your sweet semblance to some other give."**  
11. **"The eyes, 'fore duteous, now converted are / Thy unused beauty must be tomb'd with thee."**  
12. **"Flatter the mountain-tops with sovereign eye, / And all in war with Time for love of you."**  
13. **"To that sweet thief which sourly robs from me."**  
14. **"Theirs for their style I'll read, his for his love."**  
15. **"Make war upon this bloody tyrant, Time?"**

    *Theme: The speaker’s unwavering devotion despite the beloved’s flaws.*

**1.**  
*And fortify yourself in your decay*  
*And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:*  
*Calls back the lovely April of her prime:*  
*Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother.*  

**2.**  
*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*Who all their parts of me to thee did give;*  
*If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:*  
*Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?*  

**3.**  
*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*  
*Yet doth it steal sweet hours from love's delight.*  

**4.**  
*An eye more bright than theirs, less false in rolling,*  
*Do in consent shake hands to torture me;*  
*And from the forlorn world his visage hide,*  
*And your sweet semblance to some other give.*  

**5.**  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise.*

*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*Who all their parts of me to thee did give.*  

**7.**  
*For never-resting time leads summer on*  
*Or ten times happier, be it ten for one;*  
*That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows*  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?*  

**8.**  
*O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary*  
*Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit,*  
*If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:*  
*So long as youth and thou are of one date.*  

**9.**  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase,*  
*To say, within thine own deep-sunken eyes,*  
*That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows*  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night,*  
*And sable curls all silver'd o'er with white;*  
*My love shall in my verse ever live young.*  

**10.**  
*Resembling sire and child and happy mother*  
*Which husbandry in honour might uphold*  
*You had a father: let your son say so.*  

**11.**  
*Flatter the mountain-tops with sovereign eye,*  
*If all were minded so, the times should cease*  
*But since she prick'd thee out for women's pleasure,*  
*Is from the book of honour razed quite.*  

**12.**  
*For whether beauty, birth, or wealth, or wit,*  
*And though they be outstripp'd by every pen,*  
*With shifting change, as is false women's fashion;*  
*Is from the book of honour razed quite.*  

**13.**  
*The offender's sorrow lends but weak relief*  
*Make sweet some vial; treasure thou some place*  
*The world will be thy widow and still weep*  
*Who all their parts of me to thee did give.*  

**14.**  
*If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:*  
*Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?*  

**15.**  
*Flowers distill'd though they with winter meet,*  
*If from thyself to store thou wouldst convert;*  
*Unthrifty loveliness, why dost thou spend*

*But that I hope some good conceit of thine*  
*Which erst from heat did canopy the herd,*  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase.*

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, self-reflection, and the tension between ideal beauty and reality:

1. **Self-Doubt and Ideal Beauty**  
   *"Shall hate be fairer lodged than gentle love?*  
   *"Thy unused beauty must be tomb'd with thee,*  
   *"Within thine own bud buriest thy content."*

2. **Conflict with Time and Mortality**  
   *"Nor can thy shame give physic to my grief;*  
   *"And every fair with his fair doth rehearse,*  
   *"Or else receivest with pleasure thine annoy?"*

3. **Love as a Battle Against Time**  
   *"Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion's paws,*  
   *"When forty winters shall beseige thy brow,*  
   *"Gilding pale streams with heavenly alchemy."*

4. **The Speaker's Self-Deprecation**  
   *"I make my love engrafted to this store:*  
   *"Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame,*  
   *"And nothing 'gainst Time's scythe can make defence."*

5. **Eternal Presence and Beauty**  
   *"Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?*  
   *"Which hides your life and shows not half your parts?"*  
   *"But that I hope some good conceit of thine"*  
   *"And being frank she lends to those are free."

6. **The Speaker's Hope for the Beloved**  
   *"O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,*  
   *"Will be a tatter'd weed, of small worth held:*  
   *"That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows"*  
   *"And perspective it is the painter's art."

7. **Love as a Sacred Act**  
   *"Serving with looks his sacred majesty;"*  
   *"For at a frown they in their glory die."*

8. **The Speaker's Reflection on Time**  
   *"If the true concord of well-tuned sounds,*  
   *"Sweets with sweets war not, joy delights in joy."*

9. **The Beloved's Beauty and the Speaker's Longing**  
   *"As any mother's child, though not so bright,*  
   *"By oft predict that I in heaven find:*  
   *"For thee and for myself no quiet find."

10. **The Speaker's Self-Compassion**  
    *"I make my love engrafted to this store:*  
    *"Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame,*  
    *"And nothing 'gainst Time's scythe can make defence."

These lines encapsulate the poem's themes of love, self-awareness, the passage of time, and the struggle between ideal beauty and reality. They highlight the speaker's internal conflict, their longing for the beloved, and the tension between fleeting beauty and enduring love.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and the enduring nature of affection:

---

**1.**  
*Shall sum my count and make my old excuse,*  
*And by addition me of thee defeated,*  
*With sun and moon, with earth and sea's rich gems,*  
*How can I then be elder than thou art?*  

**2.**  
*With April's first-born flowers, and all things rare,*  
*That 'gainst thyself thou stick'st not to conspire.*  

**3.**  
*Yet mortal looks adore his beauty still,*  
*To the wide world and all her fading sweets.*  

**4.**  
*When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,*  
*That use is not forbidden usury,*  
*The sad account of fore-bemoaned moan,*  
*Disdains the tillage of thy husbandry?*  

**5.**  
*So long as men can breathe or eyes can see,*  
*In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear.*  

**6.**  
*With beauty's treasure, ere it be self-kill'd,*  
*But do not so; I love thee in such sort*  
*When every private widow well may keep*  
*Although our undivided loves are one:*  

**7.**  
*With virtuous wish would bear your living flowers,*  
*O, learn to read what silent love hath writ:*  
*In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:*  
*Sap cheque'd with frost and lusty leaves quite gone,*  
*And shalt by fortune once more re-survey*  
*As truth and beauty shall together thrive,*  
*Then, were not summer's distillation left,*  
*As he takes from you, I engraft you new.*  

**8.**  
*Sings this to thee: 'thou single wilt prove none.'*  
*Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date.*  

**9.**  
*To see his active child do deeds of youth,*  
*By unions married, do offend thine ear,*  
*Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth.*  

**10.**  
*And that fresh blood which youngly thou bestowest*  
*That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows*  
*Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother.*  

**11.**  
*And threescore year would make the world away.*  

**12.**  
*All losses are restored and sorrows end.*  

**13.**  
*Without thy help by me be borne alone.*  
*And shalt by fortune once more re-survey*  
*Save breed, to brave him when he takes thee hence.*  

**14.**  
*Grant, if thou wilt, thou art beloved of many,*  
*But things removed that hidden in thee lie!*  

**15.**  
*When I consider every thing that grows*  
*These poor rude lines of thy deceased lover,*  
*The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now.*  

**16.**  
*Even so my sun one early morn did shine*

**25.**  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*Thou shouldst print more, not let that copy die.*

*By oft predict that I in heaven find:*

*When sparkling stars twire not thou gild'st the even.*  

**30.**  
*All men make faults, and even I in this,*  
*Now stand you on the top of happy hours,*  
*Thy adverse party is thy advocate--*  

**31.**  
*Find no determination: then you were*  
*Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise.*  

**32.**  
*To march in ranks of better equipage:*  
*Beauty o'ersnow'd and bareness every where:*  

**33.**  
*And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill.*  

**34.**  
*Despite of wrinkles this thy golden time.*  

**35.**  
*That's for thyself to breed another thee,*  
*But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,*  
*O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!*

*I may not evermore acknowledge thee

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and the tension between fleeting moments and enduring legacy:

1. **"Or ten times happier, be it ten for one;**  
   *Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?*"  

2. **"Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread**  
   *And dumb presagers of my speaking breast,*  
   *Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise."*  

3. **"Though in our lives a separable spite,**  
   *And dost him grace when clouds do blot the heaven:"**  

4. **"Upon thyself thy beauty's legacy?**  
   *That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:"**  

5. **"And make me travel forth without my cloak,**  
   *Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise."*  

6. **"With April's first-born flowers, and all things rare**  
   *Anon permit the basest clouds to ride*  
   *To work my mind, when body's work's expired:"**  

7. **"Kissing with golden face the meadows green,**  
   *If thou survive my well-contented day,*  
   *To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way,*  
   *Excusing thy sins more than thy sins are;"**  

8. **"Holds in perfection but a little moment,**  
   *Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion's paws,*  
   *Lord of my love, to whom in vassalage*  
   *Do in consent shake hands to torture me;"**  

9. **"Ah! but those tears are pearl which thy love sheds,**  
   *Nor it nor no remembrance what it was:"**  

10. **"To him that bears the strong offence's cross,**  
    *To witness duty, not to show my wit:"**  

11. **"For having traffic with thyself alone,**  
    *From sullen earth, sings hymns at heaven's gate;"**  

12. **"Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee:**  
    *For through the painter must you see his skill,*  
    *To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way,*  
    *Let those whom Nature hath not made for store,*  
    *Harsh featureless and rude, barrenly perish:"**  

13. **"When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,**  
    *Harsh featureless and rude, barrenly perish:"**  

14. **"Or else receivest with pleasure thine annoy?"**  

15. **"Wishing me like to one more rich in hope,**  
    *Although our undivided loves are one:"**  

16. **"Find no determination: then you were**  
    *Yourself again after yourself's decease:"**  

17. **"So is it not with me as with that Muse**  
    *So thou through windows of thine age shall see,*  
    *But then begins a journey in my head,*  
    *Which, used, lives th' executor to be."**  

18. **"So is it not with me as with that Muse**  
    *Looking on darkness which the blind do see,*  
    *Thou gavest me thine, not to give back again."**  

19. **"Thy beauty's form in table of my heart;"**  
    *But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,*  
    *But thou, contracted to thine own bright eyes,*  
    *When I do count the clock that tells the time,*  
    *For beauty's pattern to succeeding men."**  

20. **"In our two loves there is but one respect,**  
    *Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise."**

1. **"Looking on darkness which the blind do see / Holds in perfection but a little moment,"**  
2. **"Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel."**

4. **"For at a frown they in their glory die."**

7. **"Thy beauty's form in table of my heart; / Presume not on thy heart when mine is slain."**

13. **"Ten times thyself were happier than thou art."**  
14. **"With sun and moon, with earth and sea's rich gems, / My love shall in my verse ever live young."**  
15. **"Unless thou take that honour from thy name: / Thy beauty's form in table of my heart."**  
16. **"Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth."**  
17. **"How can I then be elder than thou art?"**  
18. **"For then my thoughts, from far where I abide, / Now see what good turns eyes for eyes have done."**  
19. **"My glass shall not persuade me I am old, / But as the marigold at the sun's eye."**

24. **"But day doth daily draw my sorrows longer / When, in disgrace with fortune and men's eyes, / Thou art the grave where buried love doth live."**  
25. **"As an unperfect actor on the stage / When forty winters shall beseige thy brow, / That on himself such murderous shame commits."**  
26. **"And kept unused, the user so destroys it."**  
27. **"Is but the seemly raiment of my heart, / Unless thou take that honour from thy name."**  
28. **"To that sweet thief which sourly robs from me."**  
29. **"As an unperfect actor on the stage / Shifts but his place, for still the world enjoys it."**  
30. **"And sable curls all silver'd o'er with white; / Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue."**

    To eat the world's due, by the grave and thee."**

    And that unfair which fairly doth excel."**  
    (The speaker’s acknowledgment of flaws and the unfairness of perception.)  

These lines weave together themes of love, time, beauty, and the tension between appearance and reality, capturing the poem’s emotional depth and philosophical inquiry.<|im_end|>

Here are **selected lines** from the poem, capturing its themes of love, beauty, determination, and the passage of time:

---

**1.**  
*And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:*  
*Which happies those that pay the willing loan;*  
*Excusing thy sins more than thy sins are;*  
*Find no determination: then you were.*  

**2.**  
*Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts,*  
*Find no determination: then you were.*  

**3.**  
*If all were minded so, the times should cease,*  
*Roses have thorns, and silver fountains mud;*  
*Hung with the trophies of my lovers gone,*  
*Shall hate be fairer lodged than gentle love?*  

**4.**  
*Look in thy glass, and tell the face thou viewest,*  
*And there reigns love and all love's loving parts,*  
*As fast as thou shalt wane, so fast thou growest.*  

**5.**  
*And in mine own love's strength seem to decay,*  
*All men make faults, and even I in this,*  
*O' let me, true in love, but truly write,*  
*After a thousand victories once foil'd.*  

**6.**  
*From his low tract and look another way:*  
*Hast thou, the master-mistress of my passion?*  

**7.**  
*But when from highmost pitch, with weary car,*  
*Pity the world, or else this glutton be,*  
*This wish I have; then ten times happy me!*  

**8.**  
*FROM fairest creatures we desire increase,*  
*Then of thy beauty do I question make,*  
*Is but the seemly raiment of my heart,*  
*Which in my bosom's shop is hanging still.*  

**9.**  
*And all those friends which I thought buried.*  

**10.**  
*Make thee another self, for love of me,*  
*That use is not forbidden usury,*  
*And your true rights be term'd a poet's rage.*  

**11.**  
*And in fresh numbers number all your graces,*  
*You should live twice; in it and in my rhyme.*  

**12.**  
*Theirs for their style I'll read, his for his love.*  

**13.**  
*But since she prick'd thee out for women's pleasure,*  
*Thou art more lovely and more temperate:*  
*Nor can thy shame give physic to my grief;*  
*Shall sum my count and make my old excuse?*  

**14.**  
*Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*  
*Whose strength's abundance weakens his own heart.*  

**15.**  
*And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill.*  

**16.**  
*Look, whom she best endow'd she gave the more;*  
*Will be a tatter'd weed, of small worth held:*  
*No longer yours than you yourself here live:*  
*Or say with princes if it shall go well,*  
*Or ten times happier, be it ten for one.*  

**17.**  
*From sullen earth, sings hymns at heaven's gate,*  
*Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind,*  
*For then my thoughts, from far where I abide,*  
*So is it not with me as with that Muse*  
*Till then not show my head where thou mayst prove me.*  

**18.**  
*Let those whom Nature hath not made for store,*  
*And 'gainst myself a lawful plea commence:*  
*If I could write the beauty of your eyes*  
*Which this, Time's pencil, or my pupil pen,*  
*Much liker than your painted counterfeit:*  

**19.**  
*And night doth nightly make grief's strength seem stronger.*  

**20.**  
*As those gold candles fix'd in heaven's air:*  
*Make thee another self, for love of me,*  
*All men make faults, and even I in this,*  
*Where all the treasure of thy lusty days,*  
*And your sweet semblance to some other give.*  

---

Here are selected lines from the poem, highlighting themes of love, beauty, time, self-deception, and the speaker's longing for the beloved:

---

**1.**  
*Of public honour and proud titles boast,*  
*Who will believe my verse in time to come,*  
*For beauty's pattern to succeeding men.*  

**2.**  
*That I in thy abundance am sufficed,*  
*Since sweets and beauties do themselves forsake,*  
*Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*  
*The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell,*  
*Thou of thyself thy sweet self dost deceive.*  

**3.**  
*In our two loves there is but one respect,*  
*Anon permit the basest clouds to ride*  
*Grant, if thou wilt, thou art beloved of many,*  
*Hath dear religious love stol'n from mine eye*  
*The dear repose for limbs with travel tired.*  

**4.**  
*Which husbandry in honour might uphold*  
*Shall sum my count and make my old excuse,*  
*Which this, Time's pencil, or my pupil pen,*  
*But when from highmost pitch, with weary car,*  
*When I do count the clock that tells the time,*  
*O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!*  

**5.**  
*I may not evermore acknowledge thee,*  
*For no man well of such a salve can speak*  
*And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill.*  

**6.**  
*Make the earth devour her own sweet brood,*  
*When I consider every thing that grows*  
*Make sweet some vial; treasure thou some place*  
*Within thine own bud buriest thy content*  
*The bounteous largess given thee to give?*  

**7.**  
*That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:*  
*Thou art thy mother's glass, and she in thee*  
*For having traffic with thyself alone,*  
*How far I toil, still farther off from thee.*  

**8.**  
*When forty winters shall beseige thy brow,*  
*Hast thou, the master-mistress of my passion,*  
*To hideous winter and confounds him there,*  
*But if thou live, remember'd not to be,*  
*But as the marigold at the sun's eye,*  
*Profitless usurer, why dost thou use*  
*As I, not for myself, but for thee will,*  
*Though thou repent, yet I have still the loss:*  

**9.**  
*How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use,*  
*And many maiden gardens yet unset*  
*To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way,*  
*And weep afresh love's long since cancell'd woe,*  
*Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion's paws,*  
*But day doth daily draw my sorrows longer*  
*How far I toil, still farther off from thee.*  

**10.**  
*Kissing with golden face the meadows green,*  
*Theirs for their style I'll read, his for his love.'*  

**11.**  
*And that fresh blood which youngly thou bestowest*  
*Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate*  
*In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:*  

**12.**  
*O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,*  
*Haply I think on thee, and then my state,*  
*With what I most enjoy contented least:*  

**13.**  
*Nor can thy shame give physic to my grief,*  
*To give away yourself keeps yourself still,*  
*As tender nurse her babe from faring ill.*  

**14.**  
*Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age,*  
*Resembling strong youth in his middle age,*  
*Within thine own bud buriest thy content*  
*Reserve them for my love, not for their rhyme,*  
*And all the rest forgot for which he toil'd:*  

**15.**  
*Feed'st thy light'st flame with self-substantial fuel,*  
*Which I new pay as if not paid before.*  

**16.**  
*Anon permit the basest clouds to ride*  
*Presents thy shadow to my sightless view,*  
*Gilding the object whereupon it gazeth,*  
*But thou, contracted to thine own bright eyes,*  
*A woman's face with Nature's own hand painted*  
*Make war upon this bloody tyrant, Time?*  

**17.**  
*And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill.*  

**18.**  
*When lofty trees I see barren of leaves*  
*For thee and for myself no quiet find.*  

**19.**  
*Hung with the trophies of my lovers gone,*  
*That hath his windows glazed with thine eyes.*  

**20.**  
*And for a woman wert thou first created,*  
*Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard,*  
*Bearing thy heart, which I will keep so chary*  
*If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:*  

**21.**  
*When to the sessions of sweet silent thought*  
*O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,*  
*When I do count the clock that tells the time,*  
*So should the lines of life that life repair,*  

**22.**  
*Or any of these all, or all, or more,*  
*To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir.*  

---

These lines capture the poem's emotional depth, metaphors, and themes of love, beauty, and time. The selected lines emphasize the speaker's longing, self-deception, and the transient nature of beauty and love.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and the eternal struggle against decay:

---

**1.**  
*How can I then return in happy plight,*  
*And having climb'd the steep-up heavenly hill,*  
*O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,*  
*Now stand you on the top of happy hours,*  
*Compare them with the bettering of the time,*  
*Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*  
*Pluck the keen teeth from the fierce tiger's jaws,*  
*Makes black night beauteous and her old face new.*  

**2.**  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live,*  
*Thy beauty's form in table of my heart,*  
*Which I new pay as if not paid before.*  

**3.**  
*Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date.*  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live,*  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident.*  

**4.**  
*For to thy sensual fault I bring in sense,*  
*And die as fast as they see others grow.*  

**5.**  
*Haply I think on thee, and then my state,*  
*To show me worthy of thy sweet respect:*  
*Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow,*  
*Without this, folly, age and cold decay:*  
*But day by night, and night by day, oppress'd?*  

**6.**  
*Which to repair should be thy chief desire.*  

**7.**  
*If it were fill'd with your most high deserts,*  
*May make seem bare, in wanting words to show it,*  
*As interest of the dead, which now appear*  
*To find where your true image pictured lies.*  

**8.**  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase,*  
*To see his active child do deeds of youth,*  
*Then happy I, that love and am beloved*  
*Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most.*  

**9.**  
*But if the while I think on thee, dear friend,*  
*Duty so great, which wit so poor as mine*  
*If from thyself to store thou wouldst convert,*  
*Then look I death my days should expiate.*  

**10.**  
*O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary*  
*Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart,*  
*But wherefore do not you a mightier way*  
*Die single, and thine image dies with thee.*  

**11.**  
*How can I then be elder than thou art?*  
*Are windows to my breast, where-through the sun*  
*Where wasteful Time debateth with Decay,*  
*A man in hue, all 'hues' in his controlling,*  
*You should live twice; in it and in my rhyme.*  

**12.**  
*Of his self-love, to stop posterity?*  
*O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:*  
*And in fresh numbers number all your graces,*  
*Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame,*  
*Make glad and sorry seasons as thou fleets,*  
*Thy youth's proud livery, so gazed on now,*  
*For all that beauty that doth cover thee*  
*Let me confess that we two must be twain,*  
*Vaunt in their youthful sap, at height decrease,*  
*But day by night, and night by day, oppress'd?*  

**13.**  
*But that I hope some good conceit of thine*  
*Nor it nor no remembrance what it was:*  
*And puts apparel on my tatter'd loving,*  
*Whilst I, whom fortune of such triumph bars,*  
*Which, like a jewel hung in ghastly night,*  
*Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,*  
*Which to repair should be thy chief desire.*  

**14.**  
*For having traffic with thyself alone,*  
*For precious friends hid in death's dateless night,*  
*Lifts up his burning head, each under eye*  
*For then my thoughts, from far where I abide,*  
*More than that tongue that more hath more express'd.*  

**15.**  
*If I could write the beauty of your eyes*  
*The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now.*  
*If I could write the beauty of your eyes*  
*And see thy blood warm when thou feel'st it cold.*  

**16.**  
*So shall those blots that do with me remain*  
*Whilst that this shadow doth such substance give*  
*When day's oppression is not eased by night,*  
*Do in consent shake hands to torture me,*  
*And see the brave day sunk in hideous night,*  
*And in fresh numbers number all your graces,*  
*Resembling sire and child and happy mother*  
*Then let not winter's ragged hand deface*  
*The age to come would say 'This poet lies:'*  
*O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might.*  

**17.**  
*The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell,*  
*Then may I dare to boast how I do love thee;*  
*Now is the time that face should form another,*  
*Such heavenly touches ne'er touch'd earthly faces.'*  

**18.**  
*Grant, if thou wilt, thou art beloved of many,*  
*Why lovest thou that which thou receivest not gladly,*  
*And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:*  
*Myself corrupting, salving thy amiss,*  
*And 'gainst myself a lawful plea commence:*  
*Much steals men's eyes and women's souls amazeth.*  

**19.**  
*Lo! in the orient when the gracious light*  
*Making a couplement of proud compare,*  
*If from thyself to store thou wouldst convert,*  
*For thee and for myself no quiet find.*  

**20.**  
*Whose fresh repair if now thou not renewest,*  
*For having traffic with thyself alone,*  
*So I, made lame by fortune's dearest spite,*  
*Then look I death my days should expiate.*

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, and the passage of time, along with some of the more poignant or metaphorical expressions:

---

**1.**  
*Thou that art now the world's fresh ornament*  
*Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee:*  

**2.**  
*If it were fill'd with your most high deserts?*  
*To hear with eyes belongs to love's fine wit.*  

**3.**  
*My glass shall not persuade me I am old,*  
*Who will believe my verse in time to come,*  
*Exceeded by the height of happier men.*  

**4.**  
*Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue,*  
*Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*  
*When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  
*Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread*  
*And in fresh numbers number all your graces,*  
*Calls back the lovely April of her prime:*  

**5.**  
*Hung with the trophies of my lovers gone,*  
*And shalt by fortune once more re-survey*  
*Holds in perfection but a little moment,*  
*Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,*  
*By unions married, do offend thine ear,*  
*Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,*  
*Whose speechless song, being many, seeming one,*  
*So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night,*  
*Much liker than your painted counterfeit:*  

**6.**  
*When that churl Death my bones with dust shall cover,*  
*The dear repose for limbs with travel tired,*  
*Holds in perfection but a little moment,*  
*Which erst from heat did canopy the herd,*  
*So great a sum of sums, yet canst not live?*  

**7.**  
*That I in thy abundance am sufficed*  
*Which husbandry in honour might uphold*  
*But if the while I think on thee, dear friend,*  
*Whilst I, whom fortune of such triumph bars,*  
*Points on me graciously with fair aspect*  
*I may not evermore acknowledge thee,*  
*Which happies those that pay the willing loan:*  

**8.**  
*O, let my books be then the eloquence*  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*And loathsome canker lives in sweetest bud.*  

**9.**  
*Who for thyself art so unprovident.*  
*Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow,*  
*Die single, and thine image dies with thee.*  

**10.**  
*Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit,*  
*Nor thou with public kindness honour me,*  
*Without thy help by me be borne alone.*  

**11.**  
*Ten times thyself were happier than thou art,*  
*Neither in inward worth nor outward fair,*  
*But thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,*  
*When I perceive that men as plants increase,*  
*And in mine own love's strength seem to decay,*  
*Pointing to each his thunder, rain and wind,*  
*More than that tongue that more hath more express'd.*  

**12.**  
*Which bounteous gift thou shouldst in bounty cherish:*  
*Makes black night beauteous and her old face new.*  

**13.**  
*Then let not winter's ragged hand deface*  
*But flowers distill'd though they with winter meet,*  
*Why lovest thou that which thou receivest not gladly,*  
*With what I most enjoy contented least:*  

**14.**  
*Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind,*  
*Kissing with golden face the meadows green,*  
*Pluck the keen teeth from the fierce tiger's jaws,*  
*In our two loves there is but one respect,*  
*Let them say more than like of hearsay well:*  

**15.**  
*So is it not with me as with that Muse*  
*I summon up remembrance of things past,*  
*Nor it nor no remembrance what it was:*  
*Points on me graciously with fair aspect*  
*If all were minded so, the times should cease*  
*And all the rest forgot for which he toil'd:*  

**16.**  
*And for a woman wert thou first created;*  
*Or ten times happier, be it ten for one,*  
*Why didst thou promise such a beauteous day,*  
*Like to the lark at break of day arising*  
*And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
*Whose fresh repair if now thou not renewest,*  
*And by addition me of thee defeated,*  
*Great princes' favourites their fair leaves spread*  
*Which I new pay as if not paid before.*  

---

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and the enduring nature of affection, along with their emotional depth and poetic imagery:

---

**1.**  
*The one by toil, the other to complain*  
*Is it for fear to wet a widow's eye?*  
*To that sweet thief which sourly robs from me.*  

**2.**  
*Though yet, heaven knows, it is but as a tomb*  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live,*  
*And with old woes new wail my dear time's waste:*  

**3.**  
*And do whate'er thou wilt, swift-footed Time,*  
*When I behold the violet past prime,*  
*Yet doth it steal sweet hours from love's delight.*  

**4.**  
*Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most.*  
*Which, like a jewel hung in ghastly night,*  
*Bearing thy heart, which I will keep so chary*  
*That beauty still may live in thine or thee.*  

**5.**  
*To hear with eyes belongs to love's fine wit.*  
*Unless thou take that honour from thy name:*  
*But thou, contracted to thine own bright eyes,*  
*To hideous winter and confounds him there;*  
*To find where your true image pictured lies,*  
*And make me travel forth without my cloak,*  
*Then, beauteous niggard, why dost thou abuse*  
*Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,*  
*Duty so great, which wit so poor as mine*  
*Who plead for love and look for recompense*  
*And fortify yourself in your decay*  
*So long as youth and thou are of one date;*  

**6.**  
*Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth.*  
*But thy eternal summer shall not fade*  
*Compare them with the bettering of the time,*  
*With means more blessed than my barren rhyme?*  

**7.**  
*But that thou none lovest is most evident,*  
*Who all their parts of me to thee did give,*  
*That 'gainst thyself thou stick'st not to conspire.*  

**8.**  
*With ugly rack on his celestial face,*  
*If all were minded so, the times should cease*  
*Vaunt in their youthful sap, at height decrease,*  
*Full many a glorious morning have I seen*  
*But if thou live, remember'd not to be,*  
*Whose strength's abundance weakens his own heart.*  

**9.**  
*Beauty o'ersnow'd and bareness every where:*  
*Or any of these all, or all, or more,*  
*Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee:*  

**10.**  
*And each, though enemies to either's reign,*  
*Then of thy beauty do I question make,*  
*Yet him for this my love no whit disdaineth.*  

**11.**  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
*Wishing me like to one more rich in hope,*  
*Seeking that beauteous roof to ruinate*  
*Lord of my love, to whom in vassalage*  
*In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear.*  

**12.**  
*Calls back the lovely April of her prime:*  
*Against the stormy gusts of winter's day*  
*That then I scorn to change my state with kings.*  

**13.**  
*As I, not for myself, but for thee will;*  
*With all triumphant splendor on my brow;*  
*Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee:*  

**14.**  
*But out, alack! he was but one hour mine;*  
*O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:*  
*Who heaven itself for ornament doth use*  
*This were to be new made when thou art old,*  
*Nor it nor no remembrance what it was:*

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, time, beauty, and enduring devotion:

1. **"Gilding the object whereupon it gazeth;  
   Thou art more lovely and more temperate;"**  
   *Themes: Beauty, admiration, and the fleeting nature of time.*

2. **"If ten of thine ten times refigured thee;  
   If thou survive my well-contented day;"**  
   *Themes: Transience of beauty, the speaker’s longing for the beloved’s eternal presence.*

3. **"Thy beauty's form in table of my heart;  
   All naked, will bestow it;"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s devotion and the idea of love enduring beyond death.*

4. **"But forty winters shall beseige thy brow;  
   Sap checked with frost and lusty leaves quite gone;"**  
   *Themes: Time’s cruel impact on the beloved’s beauty.*

5. **"Thy adverse party is thy advocate;  
   And dost him grace when clouds do blot the heaven;"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s defiance of time and the beloved’s worth.*

6. **"O, learn to read what silent love hath writ;  
   Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts;"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s belief in love’s enduring power.*

7. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held;  
   Vaunt in their youthful sap, at height decrease;"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s humility and the fleeting nature of physical form.*

8. **"Thy self, contracted to thine own bright eyes,  
   With all triumphant splendor on my brow;"**  
   *Themes: The beloved’s beauty and the speaker’s admiration.*

9. **"For through the painter must you see his skill;  
   And, constant stars, in them I read such art;"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s belief in the artist’s (the beloved’s) mastery of beauty.*

10. **"So should that beauty which you hold in lease  
    Shifts but his place, for still the world enjoys it;"**  
    *Themes: The enduring value of beauty despite time.*

11. **"Myself corrupting, salving thy amiss;  
    For where is she so fair whose unear'd womb  
    Strikes each in each by mutual ordering?"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s selflessness and the beloved’s inherent beauty.*

12. **"But that I hope some good conceit of thine  
    O, that you were yourself! but, love, you are;"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s longing for the beloved’s true self.*

13. **"Whereon the stars in secret influence comment;  
    Yourself again after yourself's decease;"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s belief in love’s afterlife and enduring impact.*

14. **"Thee carved for her seal, and meant thereby  
    Gilding the object whereupon it gazeth;"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s metaphorical act of gilding the beloved’s beauty.*

15. **"Let those whom Nature hath not made for store,  
    Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame;"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s desire to protect the beloved from their own flaws.*

These lines encapsulate the poem’s exploration of love, time, and the enduring power of beauty, while reflecting the speaker’s introspection and devotion.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, time, and the struggle against mortality:

1. **"By chance or nature's changing course untrimm'd;"**  
2. **"So thou, thyself out-going in thy noon;"**  
3. **"A liquid prisoner pent in walls of glass;"**  
4. **"Ah! if thou issueless shalt hap to die."**  
5. **"The age to come would say 'This poet lies:'"**  
6. **"I all alone beweep my outcast state."**  
7. **"To thee I send this written embassage;"**  
8. **"I will not praise that purpose not to sell."**  
9. **"O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might."**  
10. **"Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard."**  
11. **"Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?"**  
12. **"That I in thy abundance am sufficed."**  
13. **"Make war upon this bloody tyrant, Time?"**  
14. **"Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart?"**  
15. **"Then, were not summer's distillation left,"**  
16. **"Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow?"**  
17. **"The offender's sorrow lends but weak relief."**  
18. **"With what I most enjoy contented least;"**  
19. **"That this huge stage presenteth nought but shows."**  
20. **"By unions married, do offend thine ear."**  
21. **"To say, within thine own deep-sunken eyes,"**

23. **"And loathsome canker lives in sweetest bud."**  
24. **"Nor thou with public kindness honour me,"**

33. **"Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth."**  
34. **"Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May."**  
35. **"Is it for fear to wet a widow's eye?"**  
36. **"Which husbandry in honour might uphold."**  
37. **"And trouble deal heaven with my bootless cries."**  
38. **"With beauty's treasure, ere it be self-kill'd."**  
39. **"And summer's green all girded up in sheaves."**

50. **"That I an accessary needs must be."**

    (Themes: Time’s relentless impact, the speaker’s longing for the beloved’s presence.)

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of beauty, time, love, and the fleeting nature of appearance:

1. **"Yet mortal looks adore his beauty still,"**  
2. **"Thou of thyself thy sweet self dost deceive."**  
3. **"Find no determination: then you were"**  
4. **"Can make you live yourself in eyes of men."**  
5. **"Thou dost beguile the world, unbless some mother."**  
6. **"No longer yours than you yourself here live:"**  
7. **"Entitled in thy parts do crowned sit."**  
8. **"O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,"**  
9. **"For where is she so fair whose unear'd womb"**  
10. **"But since she prick'd thee out for women's pleasure,"**  
11. **"But out, alack! he was but one hour mine;"**  
12. **"To be death's conquest and make worms thine heir."**  
13. **"The world will be thy widow and still weep"**  
14. **"Or some fierce thing replete with too much rage."**  
15. **"For whether beauty, birth, or wealth, or wit,"**  
16. **"Wishing me like to one more rich in hope."**  
17. **"But flowers distill'd though they with winter meet,"**  
18. **"And there reigns love and all love's loving parts."**  
19. **"Which to repair should be thy chief desire."**  
20. **"But flowers distill'd though they with winter meet,"**  
21. **"And there reigns love and all love's loving parts."**  
22. **"Which in thy breast doth live, as thine in me."**  
23. **"That am debarr'd the benefit of rest?"**  
24. **"O, let my books be then the eloquence"**  
25. **"That use is not forbidden usury,"**  
26. **"Presents thy shadow to my sightless view."**  
27. **"Strikes each in each by mutual ordering,"**  
28. **"Mine eye hath play'd the painter and hath stell'd"**  
29. **"How far I toil, still farther off from thee."**  
30. **"O, change thy thought, that I may change my mind!"**  
31. **"Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind,"**  
32. **"Proving his beauty by succession thine!"**  
33. **"Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?"**  
34. **"Doth homage to his new-appearing sight."**  
35. **"Or ten times happier, be it ten for one?"**  

These lines emphasize the tension between external beauty and internal truth, the fleeting nature of time, and the enduring power of love. They reflect the poem’s themes of transience, the struggle between appearance and reality, and the complexity of human emotion.<|im_end|>

Here are several lines from the poem that highlight themes of love, beauty, enduring devotion, and the struggle against time, along with their emotional and thematic significance:

---

**1.**  
*Nor lose possession of that fair thou owest;*  
*To him that bears the strong offence's cross.*  
*(Direct expression of love and the speaker's claim to the beloved's beauty.)*

**2.**  
*Within thine own bud buriest thy content,*  
*And moan the expense of many a vanish'd sight.*  
*(Metaphor for the beloved's hidden sorrow and the speaker's longing.)*

**3.**  
*The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now.*  
*To let base clouds o'ertake me in my way.*  
*(Contrast between the beloved's hidden presence and the speaker's vulnerability.)*

**4.**  
*Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel.*  
*(The beloved's dual nature as both enemy and beloved, reflecting the speaker's paradoxical love.)*

**5.**  
*Die single, and thine image dies with thee.*  
*But do not so; I love thee in such sort*  
*With sun and moon, with earth and sea's rich gems,*  
*Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise.*  
*(The speaker's declaration of love as eternal and transcendent, surpassing mortal limitations.)*

**6.**  
*Leaving thee living in posterity?*  
*And, tender churl, makest waste in niggarding.*  
*(Critique of the beloved's indifference, yet a plea for enduring love.)*

**7.**  
*Making a famine where abundance lies,*  
*And though they be outstripp'd by every pen,*  
*Him in thy course untainted do allow*  
*A woman's gentle heart, but not acquainted*  
*To work my mind, when body's work's expired:*  
*(The speaker's plea for the beloved's unselfishness despite their distance.)*

**8.**  
*Thou art thy mother's glass, and she in thee,*  
*But thou, contracted to thine own bright eyes,*  
*Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising,*  
*For beauty's pattern to succeeding men.*  
*(The speaker's internal conflict between love and self-disgust, yet a reaffirmation of beauty.)*

**9.**  
*And dost him grace when clouds do blot the heaven:*  
*If from thyself to store thou wouldst convert,*  
*O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might.*  
*(The speaker's assertion of love as a burden, yet a source of enduring grace.)*

**10.**  
*And there reigns love and all love's loving parts,*  
*With all triumphant splendor on my brow,*  
*As those gold candles fix'd in heaven's air:*  
*(Imagery of love as a radiant, eternal force.)*

**11.**  
*And they are rich and ransom all ill deeds.*  
*(Love as a remedy for sin and a source of redemption.)*

**12.**  
*Who will believe my verse in time to come,*  
*For at a frown they in their glory die.*  
*(The speaker's hope that love will endure despite time's decay.)*

**13.**  
*And kept unused, the user so destroys it.*  
*Then happy I, that love and am beloved*  
*Exceeded by the height of happier men.*  
*(The speaker's joy in being beloved, surpassing even the greatest heroes.)*

**14.**  
*To witness duty, not to show my wit:*  
*Though thou repent, yet I have still the loss:*  
*(The speaker's commitment to love despite the beloved's potential regret.)*

**15.**  
*But eyes this cunning want to grace their art,*  
*Delights to peep, to gaze therein on thee,*  
*Vaunt in their youthful sap, at height decrease,*  
*And stretched metre of an antique song:*  
*(The speaker's longing to see the beloved's beauty and the enduring nature of their connection.)*

**16.**  
*But things removed that hidden in thee lie!*  
*And die as fast as they see others grow.*  
*(The speaker's acknowledgment of the beloved's impermanence and the enduring nature of their love.)*

**17.**  
*If thou couldst answer 'This fair child of mine'*  
*But if thou live, remember'd not to be,*  
*For thee and for myself no quiet find.*  
*(The speaker's plea for the beloved's memory and the enduring impact of their love.)*

**18.**  
*To that sweet thief which sourly robs from me.*  
*(The beloved as a thief of the speaker's beauty and joy.)*

**19.**  
*Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel.*  
*(Repetition of the beloved's dual nature as both enemy and beloved, emphasizing the speaker's paradoxical love.)*

**20.**  
*And do whate'er thou wilt, swift-footed Time,*  
*Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date.*  
*(The speaker's acceptance of time's inevitability but the enduring power of love.)*

---

These lines capture the poem's core themes: **love as eternal, beauty as transcendent, the struggle against time, and the speaker's unwavering devotion**. They balance direct expressions of love with metaphors of nature, celestial imagery, and philosophical reflections.<|im_end|>

Here are several impactful lines from the poem, highlighting themes of love, beauty, time, and the conflict between self and other:

1. **"So is it not with me as with that Muse  
The one by toil, the other to complain"**  
   *(A metaphor for the Muse’s struggle and the speaker’s own inner turmoil.)*

2. **"Like to the lark at break of day arising  
A dearer birth than this his love had brought"**  
   *(The speaker’s own birth is compared to the lark’s awakening, emphasizing beauty and vitality.)*

3. **"If ten of thine ten times refigured thee:  
Pluck the keen teeth from the fierce tiger's jaws,  
Kissing with golden face the meadows green"**  
   *(A vivid image of transformation and beauty, contrasting the speaker’s desire for the other’s perfection.)*

4. **"But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,  
For never-resting time leads summer on"**  
   *(The inevitability of time’s relentless progress and the fleeting nature of beauty.)*

5. **"As any mother's child, though not so bright  
They draw but what they see, know not the heart"**  
   *(The speaker’s inability to fully understand the other’s beauty, mirroring the mother-child dynamic.)*

6. **"O, that you were yourself! but, love, you are  
Within thine own bud buriest thy content"**  
   *(A plea for the other to embrace their own beauty, contrasting with the speaker’s own self-doubt.)*

7. **"Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date"**  
   *(The speaker’s assertion that the other’s fate is intertwined with truth and beauty, despite their flaws.)*

8. **"But beauty's waste hath in the world an end,  
For never-resting time leads summer on"**  
   *(Repetition of the same line to emphasize the inevitability of time’s passage.)*

9. **"As I, not for myself, but for thee will;  
By chance or nature's changing course untrimm'd"**  
   *(The speaker’s love as a force beyond self, driven by destiny.)*

10. **"But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:  
'Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age'**  
    *(A direct challenge to the other’s actions, invoking the Muse as a symbol of inspiration.)*

11. **"As, thou being mine, mine is thy good report"**  
    *(The speaker’s assertion of ownership over the other’s beauty, even in their imperfections.)*

12. **"O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:  
But beauty's waste hath in the world an end"**  
    *(A plea for the speaker’s own love to transcend the fleeting nature of beauty.)*

13. **"And in themselves their pride lies buried,  
And threescore year would make the world away"**  
    *(The speaker’s reflection on the transience of pride and the world’s indifference.)*

14. **"But not to tell of good or evil luck,  
Resembling sire and child and happy mother"**  
    *(The speaker’s refusal to judge the other’s worth, likening it to a mother’s nurturing care.)*

15. **"Look, whom she best endow'd she gave the more"**  
    *(The speaker’s acknowledgment of the other’s inherent beauty, even in their flaws.)*

17. **"For whether beauty, birth, or wealth, or wit,"**  
18. **"Without this, folly, age and cold decay:"**  
19. **"Cheered and cheque'd even by the self-same sky,"**  
20. **"As tender nurse her babe from faring ill."**  
21. **"Whilst that this shadow doth such substance give"**  
22. **"Against the stormy gusts of winter's day"**  
23. **"But day doth daily draw my sorrows longer"**

38. **"When your sweet issue your sweet form should bear."**  
39. **"And in themselves their pride lies buried,"**  
40. **"So shall those blots that do with me remain"**  
41. **"Sweets with sweets war not, joy delights in joy."**

51. **"As I, not for myself, but for thee will;"**  
52. **"Pluck the keen teeth from the fierce tiger's jaws,"**

56. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held,"**

58. **"And there reigns love and all love's loving parts,"**  
59. **"Are windows to my breast, where-through the sun"**  
60. **"O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,"**

72. **"If all were minded so, the times should cease"**

</think>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, devotion, and the passage of time:

1. **"To hideous winter and confounds him there;**  
   *Neither in inward worth nor outward fair,*  
   *The sad account of fore-bemoaned moan,*  
   *To work my mind, when body's work's expired:*"  

2. **"But do not so; I love thee in such sort,**  
   *Though yet, heaven knows, it is but as a tomb,*  
   *Look, what an unthrift in the world doth spend,*  
   *And wear their brave state out of memory;"*  

3. **"The sad account of fore-bemoaned moan,**  
   *As tender nurse her babe from faring ill."*  

4. **"Then let not winter's ragged hand deface**  
   *Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit,*  
   *I tell the day, to please them thou art bright,*  
   *Then of thy beauty do I question make,*  
   *To change your day of youth to sullied night;"*  

5. **"And from the forlorn world his visage hide,**  
   *That beauty still may live in thine or thee."*  

6. **"When day's oppression is not eased by night,**  
   *And night doth nightly make grief's strength seem stronger."*  

7. **"Borne on the bier with white and bristly beard,**  
   *Thy beauty's form in table of my heart;"*  

8. **"And by addition me of thee defeated,**  
   *Clouds and eclipses stain both moon and sun,*  
   *Theirs for their style I'll read, his for his love."*  

9. **"And threescore year would make the world away."**  

10. **"Full many a glorious morning have I seen,**  
    *Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
    *The painful warrior famoused for fight,*  
    *Sweets with sweets war not, joy delights in joy."*  

11. **"With means more blessed than my barren rhyme?"**  
    *Mine be thy love and thy love's use their treasure."*  

12. **"For then my thoughts, from far where I abide,**  
    *Lest my bewailed guilt should do thee shame,*  
    *Thou mayst call thine when thou from youth convertest."*  

13. **"To eat the world's due, by the grave and thee."**  

14. **"Which, like a jewel hung in ghastly night,**  
    *Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue."**  

15. **"Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age,**  
    *That's for thyself to breed another thee."**  

16. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held,"**  
    *Anon permit the basest clouds to ride,*  
    *Although our undivided loves are one:*  
    *Cheered and cheque'd even by the self-same sky."*  

17. **"And all those friends which I thought buried."**  

18. **"If thou survive my well-contented day,**  
    *The dear repose for limbs with travel tired;"*  

19. **"For to thy sensual fault I bring in sense—**  
    *Sweets with sweets war not, joy delights in joy."**  

20. **"And look upon myself and curse my fate,"**  
    *The offender's sorrow lends but weak relief,*  
    *That use is not forbidden usury,*  
    *Which in my bosom's shop is hanging still."*  

These lines emphasize the speaker's devotion, the enduring beauty of the beloved, and the tension between time, mortality, and eternal love. They reflect the poem's blend of romantic longing, metaphorical imagery, and philosophical reflection.<|im_end|>

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, time, and the enduring nature of love despite challenges:

1. **"Making a complement of proud compare,  
So thou through windows of thine age shall see"**  
   *Themes: Beauty and comparison, the passage of time.*

2. **"Sets you most rich in youth before my sight,  
For to thy sensual fault I bring in sense"**  
   *Themes: The fleeting nature of youth, the speaker’s admiration.*

3. **"O, none but unthrifts! Dear my love, you know"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s own flaws and the beloved’s enduring value.*

4. **"Make sweet some vial; treasure thou some place"**  
   *Themes: Preserving beauty, the importance of memory.*

5. **"The eyes, 'fore duteous, now converted are"**  
   *Themes: The transient nature of beauty and the speaker’s longing.*

6. **"O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s desire to cherish the beloved’s beauty.*

7. **"Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s devotion and the beloved’s worth.*

8. **"The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s longing and the beloved’s absence.*

9. **"O, let me, true in love, but truly write"**  
   *Themes: The speaker’s commitment to authenticity in love.*

10. **"Thy bosom is endeared with all hearts"**  
    *Themes: The beloved’s enduring love and the speaker’s affection.*

11. **"My glass shall not persuade me I am old"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s refusal to accept aging, the beloved’s beauty.*

12. **"The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell"**  
    *Themes: The beloved’s beauty and its impact on others.*

13. **"A liquid prisoner pent in walls of glass"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s own limitations and the beloved’s enduring beauty.*

14. **"O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s plea for love and appreciation.*

15. **"Thy adverse party is thy advocate"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s belief in the beloved’s worth despite opposition.*

16. **"The dear repose for limbs with travel tired"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s longing for the beloved’s comfort.*

17. **"And dig deep trenches in thy beauty's field"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s desire to preserve the beloved’s beauty.*

18. **"My body is the frame wherein 'tis held"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s reliance on the beloved’s beauty.*

19. **"Proving his beauty by succession thine!"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s belief in the enduring value of the beloved’s beauty.*

20. **"O, none but unthrifts! Dear my love, you know"**  
    *Themes: The speaker’s acknowledgment of the beloved’s worth despite their own flaws.*

These lines encapsulate the poem’s exploration of love, beauty, time, and the enduring power of affection, even in the face of impermanence and challenge.<|im_end|>

---

**1.**  
*Neither in inward worth nor outward fair,*  
*And yet methinks I have astronomy,*  
*Which in thy breast doth live, as thine in me:*  
*A woman's face with Nature's own hand painted*  

**2.**  
*Though in our lives a separable spite,*  
*Duty so great, which wit so poor as mine*  
*The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell,*  
*But as the riper should by time decease,*

*To change your day of youth to sullied night.*  

**3.**  
*But I forbid thee one most heinous crime:*

*When to the sessions of sweet silent thought*  
*Who plead for love and look for recompense*  
*By children's eyes her husband's shape in mind.*  

**5.**

**7.**

*O, carve not with thy hours my love's fair brow,*  
*To hear with eyes belongs to love's fine wit.*  

**12.**  
*Feed'st thy light'st flame with self-substantial fuel,*  
*That then I scorn to change my state with kings.*  

**13.**  
*Lo! thus, by day my limbs, by night my mind,*  
*In thee thy summer, ere thou be distill'd:*  
*Thou art thy mother's glass, and she in thee*  
*Sets you most rich in youth before my sight.*  

**14.**  
*Clouds and eclipses stain both moon and sun,*  
*For never-resting time leads summer on*  
*Yet, do thy worst, old Time: despite thy wrong,*  
*Calls back the lovely April of her prime:*  

**15.**  
*But as the marigold at the sun's eye,*  
*Thou mayst call thine when thou from youth convertest.*  

**16.**  
*Thou art the grave where buried love doth live,*  
*Desiring this man's art and that man's scope,*  
*In singleness the parts that thou shouldst bear.*  

**17.**  
*Then how, when nature calls thee to be gone,*  
*Of his self-love, to stop posterity?*  

**18.**  
*Proving his beauty by succession thine!*  
*And, tender churl, makest waste in niggarding.*  

**19.**  
*Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue,*  
*Which erst from heat did canopy the herd,*  
*And dig deep trenches in thy beauty's field,*  
*With April's first-born flowers, and all things rare*  

**20.**  
*And shalt by fortune once more re-survey*  
*Makes black night beauteous and her old face new.*

*Though thou repent, yet I have still the loss:*  
*Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?*  

**14.**  
*Now see what good turns eyes for eyes have done:*  
*No love toward others in that bosom sits*  
*That's for thyself to breed another thee,*  
*For having traffic with thyself alone,*  
*And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
*'Had my friend's Muse grown with this growing age,*  
*Beauty o'ersnow'd and bareness every where:'*  
*Thou mayst call thine when thou from youth convertest.*  

**15.**  
*The marigold at the sun's eye,*  
*The perfect ceremony of love's rite,*  
*And you must live, drawn by your own sweet skill.*  

---

These lines capture the poem's essence: the fleeting beauty of love, the struggle against time, and the enduring power of true love. The repeated motifs of light, death, and the contrast between beauty and time are central.<|im_end|>

15. **"Now is the time that face should form another; / And though they be outstripp'd by every pen, / And night doth nightly..."**

1. **"O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought:"**  
   *A powerful opening line that sets the tone of the poem's central theme.*

2. **"Which husbandry in honour might uphold"**  
   *A metaphor for nurturing love and honor, reflecting the speaker's devotion.*

3. **"And for a woman wert thou first created; A dearer birth than this his love had brought, More than that tongue that more hath more express'd."**  
   *Highlights the speaker's admiration for the beloved's creation and the depth of their love.*

4. **"By children's eyes her husband's shape in mind."**  
   *A poignant image of the beloved's enduring presence in the speaker's memory.*

5. **"When I consider every thing that grows O'ercharged with burden of mine own love's might."**  
   *Emphasizes the weight of the speaker's love and its overwhelming impact.*

6. **"But since he died and poets better prove, And 'gainst myself a lawful plea commence:"**  
   *A reflection on the speaker's struggle to reconcile their love with the inevitability of death.*

7. **"As fast as thou shalt wane, so fast thou growest Yet in these thoughts myself almost despising, I summon up remembrance of things past, Which to repair should be thy chief desire."**  
   *Contrasts the fleeting nature of the beloved's presence with the speaker's longing to remember them.*

8. **"And summer's lease hath all too short a date: But since he died and poets better prove, Now stand you on the top of happy hours, Save breed, to brave him when he takes thee hence."**  
   *Mimics the fleeting nature of life and the speaker's resolve to endure the beloved's absence.*

9. **"To witness duty, not to show my wit: Full many a glorious morning have I seen So flatter I the swart-complexion'd night, O, learn to read what silent love hath writ:"**  
   *A metaphor for love's quiet power and the speaker's desire to understand it.*

10. **"Is it for fear to wet a widow's eye Without thy help by me be borne alone."**  
    *A plea for the beloved's presence to comfort the speaker in their grief.*

11. **"Holds in perfection but a little moment, Serving with looks his sacred majesty; Take all my comfort of thy worth and truth."**  
    *Highlights the fleeting nature of beauty and the speaker's reliance on the beloved's worth.*

12. **"Such heavenly touches ne'er touch'd earthly faces."**  
    *A statement about the transcendent nature of love and beauty.*

13. **"Stirr'd by a painted beauty to his verse, Were an all-eating shame and thriftless praise."**  
    *A critique of how beauty is often misperceived or overestimated.*

14. **"And dumb presagers of my speaking breast, O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary, And dost him grace when clouds do blot the heaven:"**  
    *A plea for the beloved to be wise and to grace the speaker in times of adversity.*

15. **"And moan the expense of many a vanish'd sight: Thou art more lovely and more temperate: Thy end is truth's and beauty's doom and date."**  
    *A reflection on the speaker's love for the beloved and the inevitability of their loss.*

16. **"Ten times thyself were happier than thou art, And thou, all they, hast all the all of me."**  
    *A profound assertion of the speaker's love for the beloved, transcending their own existence.*

17. **"And wear their brave state out of memory; Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart, When I perceive that men as plants increase, But out, alack! he was but one hour mine; And many maiden gardens yet unset Which erst from heat did canopy the herd, But beauty's waste hath in the world an end, Holds in perfection but a little moment, And, tender churl, makest waste in niggarding."**  
    *A lament for the fleeting nature of love and the speaker's resolve to cherish the beloved's beauty.*

18. **"But do not so; I love thee in such sort, And often is his gold complexion dimm'd; Whose fresh repair if now thou not renewest, Presents thy shadow to my sightless view, Full many a glorious morning have I seen Yet eyes this cunning want to grace their art; As, thou being mine, mine is thy good report."**  
    *A declaration of the speaker's deep, unyielding love for the beloved.*

19. **"Like to the lark at break of day arising, That heaven's air in this huge rondure hems."**  
    *A metaphor for the beloved's beauty and vitality, likened to a dawn-lark.*

20. **"And weep afresh love's long since cancell'd woe, Whose speechless song, being many, seeming one, Kissing with golden face the meadows green, Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most."**

Here are several lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, beauty, time, and the enduring nature of affection:

1. **"And threescore year would make the world away."**  
2. **"To change your day of youth to sullied night;"**  
3. **"You should live twice; in it and in my rhyme."**  
4. **"Yet mortal looks adore his beauty still."**  
5. **"If I could write the beauty of your eyes / Whilst that this shadow doth such substance give / To hideous winter and confounds him there;"**  
6. **"Can make you live yourself in eyes of men."**  
7. **"But beauty's waste hath in the world an end, / Like feeble age, he reeleth from the day."**  
8. **"I may not evermore acknowledge thee, / That on himself such murderous shame commits."**  
9. **"Look, what is best, that best I wish in thee: / But if thou live, remember'd not to be."**  
10. **"She carved thee for her seal, and meant thereby / And summer's lease hath all too short a date."**  
11. **"I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought, / Or else receivest with pleasure thine annoy?"**  
12. **"Beauty o'ersnow'd and bareness every where: / That heals the wound and cures not the disgrace:"**  
13. **"For no man well of such a salve can speak / No longer yours than you yourself here live:"**  
14. **"I make my love engrafted to this store: / Let those who are in favour with their stars / Music to hear, why hear'st thou music sadly?"**  
15. **"Cheered and cheque'd even by the self-same sky, / I sigh the lack of many a thing I sought, / Or else receivest with pleasure thine annoy?"**  
16. **"All losses are restored and sorrows end."**  
17. **"O, then vouchsafe me but this loving thought: / Then can I drown an eye, unused to flow, / And threescore year would make the world away."**  
18. **"Without this, folly, age and cold decay: / This were to be new made when thou art old, / The world will be thy widow and still weep / And dig deep trenches in thy beauty's field."**  
19. **"As tender nurse her babe from faring ill."**  
20. **"Then what could death do, if thou shouldst depart, / And they are rich and ransom all ill deeds."**  
21. **"To work my mind, when body's work's expired: / By children's eyes her husband's shape in mind."**  
22. **"Whose strength's abundance weakens his own heart."**  
23. **"Though in our lives a separable spite, / Looking on darkness which the blind do see."**  
24. **"Be scorn'd like old men of less truth than tongue, / Make glad and sorry seasons as thou fleets, / And having climb'd the steep-up heavenly hill, / As those gold candles fix'd in heaven's air:"**  
25. **"They draw but what they see, know not the heart."**  

These lines emphasize the tension between fleeting beauty and enduring love, the speaker's longing for the beloved, and the paradox of time's passage. They reflect themes of mortality, the futility of aging, and the persistence of affection despite adversity.<|im_end|>

Here are selected lines from the poem that capture its themes of love, mortality, beauty, and the speaker's emotional struggle:

1. **"Delights to peep, to gaze therein on thee;**  
   *Devouring Time, blunt thou the lion's paws,*  
   *Unlook'd for joy in that I honour most.*"  
   (Themes: Time's cruelty, eternal love, and hidden joy.)

2. **"I may not evermore acknowledge thee,*  
   *When sparkling stars twire not thou gild'st the even."**  
   (The speaker’s inability to accept the beloved’s absence and the fading of beauty.)

3. **"O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary,*  
   *Profitless usurer, why dost thou use?"**  
   (A critique of love’s futility and the speaker’s self-awareness of their own corruption.)

4. **"The world will be thy widow and still weep,*  
   *Myself corrupting, salving thy amiss."**  
   (The speaker’s self-destructive love and the tragedy of losing the beloved.)

5. **"But do not so; I love thee in such sort,*  
   *Making a famine where abundance lies."**  
   (The speaker’s love as a deprivation, contrasting with the world’s abundance.)

6. **"To find where your true image pictured lies;**  
   *O, therefore, love, be of thyself so wary."**  
   (The speaker’s search for the beloved’s true self and their own caution in love.)

7. **"Thy merit hath my duty strongly knit,*  
   *Unless thou take that honour from thy name."**  
   (The speaker’s devotion to the beloved’s worth and the fear of losing their honor.)

8. **"I may not evermore acknowledge thee,*  
   *When sparkling stars twire not thou gild'st the even."**  
   (Repetition of the inability to accept the beloved’s absence, emphasizing the speaker’s grief.)

9. **"And in themselves their pride lies buried,*  
   *Or to thyself at least kind-hearted prove."**  
   (The speaker’s self-awareness of their own flaws and the need for kindness.)

10. **"So should the lines of life that life repair,*  
    *And trouble deal heaven with my bootless cries."**  
    (The speaker’s plea for life’s purpose and the futility of their cries.)

11. **"The lovely gaze where every eye doth dwell,*  
    *How much more praise deserved thy beauty's use?"**  
    (The speaker’s longing for the beloved’s beauty and the injustice of their neglect.)

12. **"And barren rage of death's eternal cold?"**  
    (The speaker’s despair at death’s indifference to love.)

13. **"I tell the day, to please them thou art bright,*  
    *Now stand you on the top of happy hours."**  
    (The speaker’s hope for the beloved’s enduring light and their own fleeting joy.)

14. **"And keep my drooping eyelids open wide,*  
    *All men make faults, and even I in this."**  
    (The speaker’s acceptance of imperfection and their own vulnerability.)

15. **"So I, for fear of trust, forget to say,*  
    *May make seem bare, in wanting words to show it."**  
    (The speaker’s self-deception to hide their love.)

16. **"But since he died and poets better prove,*  
    *Thyself thy foe, to thy sweet self too cruel."**  
    (The speaker’s realization of their own cruelty in love.)

17. **"And see thy blood warm when thou feel'st it cold."**  
    (The speaker’s hope that the beloved’s love will revive them in death.)

18. **"Without this, folly, age and cold decay:"**  
    (A warning against neglecting love, which is the root of decay.)

19. **"And from the forlorn world his visage hide,*  
    *Nor draw no lines there with thine antique pen."**  
    (The speaker’s desire to preserve the beloved’s memory and avoid betrayal.)

20. **"The region cloud hath mask'd him from me now."**  
    (The speaker’s longing for the beloved’s return and the cloud’s obscuring their vision.)

These lines capture the poem’s central themes of love’s futility, the speaker’s self-awareness, and the enduring power of beauty, even in the face of time and death.<|im_end|>
